Since 1997, the mission of Books of Discovery has been to bring health care providers around the world the very best learning tools for anatomy. know that a high school athlete in Florida, a first-tine mother in Vancouver, or a journeyman bricklayer in Liverpool has received quality care from a practitioner who has benefited from our products. We believe clear and practical information about the human body should be available to everyone - student, practitioner, patient, and layperson. It is, after all, where we live, and a better understanding can only strengthen how we move, think, and feel. This belief inspires us to serve individuals and schools all across the globe each and every day. Best wishes and happy trails. Andrew Biel, Founder Books of Discovery Books of Discovery's products are imanufactured with soy-based ink, and we strive to produce books in an environmentally friendly and socially responsible manner. Since 1997 we have donated more than $ 100,0oo to reforestation and conservation projects.

Introduction Tour Guide Tips "I kmow it like the back of my hand." Uitimately, this is what you're hoping to feel about the musculoskeletal system by the end of this book. But since we're literally on page one of our incredible journey, it might be fun to consider: “How xpuey umo 1noA mouy Aleai noA op [am Go ahead and explore your left hand, sensing what structures you feel beneath the surface. This might seem a bit silly given that you've possessed hands your entire life. But what you're likely to discover is a variety of parts and pieces, each with specific textural qualities. There are hard bits (bones), meaty seclions uey au jo sdeys peiausa aui se laa se "(suio) seaie Aqqoux pue "(saasnm) (courtesy of the fascia). Your hand also has skin, blood vessels, tendons, ligaments, and more--all of which ycu'll soon be palpating throughout the body. As you familiarize yourself with your hand, consider its tremendous functional capacity. It is the only tool that can tie shoes, caress a newborn, split a board, and perform brain surgery. The word awesome truly applies, and everything you're feeling-- in your hand, with your hand - makes it so. Consider the following questions as you explore: On your hand, where is the skin the thinnest? Thickest? Any thoughts on why your palm feels so different from the back of your hand? How many bones can you find? Can you sense where they begin and end? How much pressure (or squeezing) do you need to apply to feel things? This point will prove important later on. Do you notice the pad of muscle at the base of your thumb? Any idea why it's so big? And can you distinguish individual muscles? (Believe it or not, five muscles are packed in there.)

text: Palpation Hints Palpation means "to exaimine or explore by touching mindful, hands-on practice. This is the focus of Trai? (all organ or area of the body), usually as a diagiuostic Guide to the Body. Assessiment-the third aspect of aid." It is an art and a skill which invalves (l) locating a palpation - is a vast subject requiring a book of its own. structure, (2) becoming aware of its characteristics, and As an experience involving all the senses, palpation (3) assessing its quality or condition so you can deter- requires receptive hands and fingers, open eyes, mine how to treat it. listening ears, calm breath, and a quiet mind. As you The first two aspccts of palpation - locating and being explore ihe terrain and texlure of the bodly, be sure to attentive to the body's structures-require a thorough bring along all of your sensing tools. knowledge of funetional anatomy and experience through Making Contact Let your hands and fingers be responsive and sensitive. Relaxed, patient hands will allow the body's contours. temperature, and structures to come more easily into your awareness. For grcater sensitivity and stability, try laying one hand upon the other, using the top hand to create the necessary pressure while the bottom hand remains relaxed (0.3). This will allow the bottom hand to stay receptive as the top hand directs movement and depth. Smaller structures can be located by using one or two fingertips (0.+). Larger structures are best palpated with your whole hand. By sculpting out all of the sides and edges, full-hand contact helps to define the complete shape of a region or structure and also allows for a greater understanding of the interrelationships of structures (0.5). When palpating, you may want to close your eyes (0.6) periodically to enhance your awareness. 0.3 A firm top hand and a soft bottom hand 0.4 Using your thumbpads to explore the 0.5 Using your entire hand to small carpal bones in the wrist palpate the pelvis and sacrum palpate pal-pate L- paipare. to touch 4  Trail Guide to the Body

Working Hard vs. Working Smart Often in the excitement of trying to locate something (whether it be a nuscle or a set of car keys), you search so earnestly that your mental and physical awareness begins to diminish. Frustration arises, your breath stalls, and your hands ultimately become insensitive. You begin to 0.6 Exploring with work hard. Instead of working hard, you can work smart your eyes closed by reading the information about the structure before you palpate. Also, as you palpate, visualize vwhat you are trying to access and verbalize to your partner what you are feeling. Work smart by first localing the strueture you wish to palpate on your cwn body before palpating it on your partner's. Self-palpation will improve your kinesthetic understanding of what you are looking for on your partner. Also, read the information aloud. Hearing the language as you arc reading the text will inprove your uliderstanding and retention of the information. Lastly, be patient with your learning process. Allow yourself to "nake a wrong turn and get lost" on tlie body. Chances are you are close to what you are seeking. By letting your seuses recoguize the body's trail signs, you will get to where you want to be. Dogs, cats. horses, and other animals offer a wonderful opportunity to compare musculoskeletal anatomy through palpation. For example, the next time you are petting yaur neighbor's cat, take a mormenl to locate its scapula. Cormpare the scapula's shape, location. and surrounding tissues to those cf a human's or dog's. The anatornical cifferences rnay surprise you. but the similarities will amaze you. I can't feel it Less Is More Ugh... I can As you begin exploring the body, you might not be able to feel itl access things as readily as you wish. A common reaction is to press harder and deeper with your hands and fingers. However, instead of pushing into the muscles alnd otlher tissues, try to invite the tissues into your hands. Gentle contact will allow your hands to be sensitive, whereas excessive pushing only numbs the fingers, making for an uncomfortable experience for your partner (0.7). Even deep structures are best accessed with mild pressure. Paradoxically, the deeper you move into the body, the slower and softer your touch needs to be. Ulltimately, palpation at different levels of the body is not a question of pressure, but of intention. Having a clear intention as you scek out various structures will make for an casier, snoother jourmey. 0.7 Less is more Introduction 5

Rolling and Strumming When outlining the shape or edge of a bone, try rolling your fingers or thunb across, rather than along, its surface. 0.8 strumming your thumb across the Tlhis is sinilar to clhecking the sharpness fibers of the brachioradialis muscle of a knife by sliding your finger across the blade. Do the same with the ropy fibers of muscle tissue. Like strumming the shings of a guitar, this method uill help you ascertain the muscle's fiber direetion and tensile state (0.8). Here is a simple exercise to When you find the hair. increase your tactile sensitivity and reposition it and add palpatory skills. You will reed this another page. Continue textbook and a human hair. Lay the to add pages until you hair beneath a single page. Close can no longer locate the your eyes and palpate through the hair. How many pages page. trying to locate the hair. can you palpate through? Movement and Stillness If you were to compare the texture of newspaper with rough sandpaper, you would naturally want to rub your fingers across their surfaces. In contrast, when you lay your hand on an expectant mother's abdoinen, hoping to feel the fetus move, you naturally keep your hand still and quiet. Similarly, when you want to determine the fiber direction of a muscle or sculpt the shape of a bone, move your hands along its surface (0.9). However, when you want to feel a muscle contract or a bone move, keep your hands still and follow the movement. Put simply, if the structure you are palpating is stationary, move your hands across it; if it is moving, stay still. 0.9 Anterior view. encircling the coracoid process of the scapula with your thumb 6  Trail Guide to the Body

Movement as a Palpation Tool Throughout the text, you will be asked to create specific moveiment on a partner's body with or without that person's help. These moveiments will help to verify the location of struetures as well as any changes occurring inl the tissues as a result. Active movement is performed by your partner. He actively moves his body while you palpate or observe the movement. For example, the text may say, "Ask your partner to slowly flex his elbow while you palpate his biceps brachii muscle." All active movements performed by your partner should be slow and smooth— as changes in tissue are difficult to follow during fast, jerky motions (0.10). Sometimes you might be instructed to ask your parlmner to contract and relax a muscle. For example, "To feel the forearm flexors, lay your hand on your partner's forearm and ask hin to alternately flex and relax his wrist." 'The on-and-off aspect of this technique will not only help you 0.11 Passive flexion and extension of the shoulder locate muscles and tendons but also will give you the opportunily to feel the difference between contracted and relaxed tissue. Passive movement is the opposite of active move- ment: Your partner relaxes while you move his body. For example, when the text says, "Passively abduct and adduct the shoulder,^ you will move the arm while your partner relnains passive and allows the action to occur (0.ll). Resisted movement requires botl of you to act: Your 0.10 Active flexion and partner attempts to perform an action against your gentle extensionoftheelbow resistance. For example, "To feel the elbow flexors contract, ask your partner to flex his elbow against your resistance" (0.12). As he meets the gentle resistancc of your hand, no movement will occur at your partner's elbow. In this text, resisted movements are used to distinguish and compare the lengths, shapes, and edges of different muscle bellies and tendons. An adult has over 6c0.000 sensory receptors in the skin-more nerve endings than any other part of the body. The fingert ps are among the most sensitive areas. with up to 50.0o0 nerve endings every square inch. The fingertips are so sensitive that a single touch sensor can respond to a pressure of less than 1/1.400 of an ounce-the weighl of an average housefly. 0.i2 Resisted flexion of the elbow [ntroduction 7

When in Doubt, Ask the Body Tendon of extensor Wlhile palpating, you may be confused or have questions hallucis longus about the body's structures and their whereabouts. When in doubt, ask the body you are palpating. For cxanple, you may wonder, "What skinny tendon is this I see running along the top of thc foot?" (0.13) Thc best advice would be to follow it in both directions and see where it leads you. If it runs fron tlie big toe to the unkle alld becomes taut when the toe is extended, it is the tendon of extensor hallucis longus (p. 378). Always remember, you are never alone; the hody is waiting to help you. All of the struetures outlined in Treril Guide to fhe Body. with tlheir Latin or Greek naines, unique shapes, and buried positions, are inside you, your pariner and your patients. T'hese structures have been there for years. waiting for you to discover then. Have faith and you will be able to locate tlhem. 0.13 Exploring a skinny tendon on the dorsal surface of the foot Three Principles of Palpation 1. Move slowly. Haste onty interferes with sensation. 2. Avoid using excessive pressure. Less is truly more. BuhreisTius 3. Focus your awareness on what it is you are feeling. In other words, be present. Also. you can practice your palpation skills on yourself at any time. Yes, you may get a few curious glances. but daily routines such as waiting in line or riding the bus are wonderful opportunities to explore the malleable skin. tiny bones, and sinewy muscles of your forearms and hands. 8 Trail Guide to the Body

Learning Objectives Why are you reading Truil Guide to the Body? To develop strong palpatory anatomy skills for your hands-on modality? Maybe your instructor told you to read it? Well, in either case, it might be worthwhile to glance at Trai? Guide's learning objectives. Basically, these are the goals for you—the reader—as you explore the material. All Trail Guide will help you build your skills so you will be able to: 1. Observe the surface anatomy of the body and confidently explore the skin and fascial structures of the body. 2. Palpate the bones and bony landmarks of each body region and explore the connections between then and the soft tissues. 3. Palpate eacli umuscle fron origin lo inserlion, feeling and describing its overall shape, edges, and fiber direction(s). 4. Palpate the major joint structures, including ligaments and bursae, that are common sites of pain and injury in the region. Palpate the landmarks within each body region that identify the location of underlying nerves, blood vessels, and lymph nodes that you must be cautious of wlhen practicing manual therapy. As a manual therapist, you must be fluent in three palpatory anatomy languages. First, verbal literacy. so you can accurately docurment your rmanual therapy sessions and comrmun cate effectively with other health care professionals. Second. visual literacy. so you can observe the skin. topography. and physical contours of your clients. Third. palpatory literacy. so you can confidently assess tissue condition and provide safe and effective manual therapy. Trafl Guide will help you develop your kmowledge so you will be able to: 1. Describe the relationships between the topographical contours and underlying musculoskeletal structures, as well as the texture, thiekness, and mobility of the skin and fascial structures in each body region. 2. Nane and locate the bones, bony landmarks, and joints for each region of the body and describe the connections between them and the soft tissues of the region. 3. Nane and locate the muscles of the region, including their specific origins and insertions. 4. List alid demonstrate the action(s) of each muscle. 5. Name and locate major joint structures, including ligaments and bursae, that are common sites of pain and injury in the region. 6. Kame and locate the major nerves, blood vcssels, and lymph nodes that you must be cautious of when palpating each body region. 18 3Trail Guide to theBody

text: 1.1 Anatomical position Navigain the Body T Maps have a language all their own. Arrows point out north and south, while lines mark roads and latitudes. These icons provide users with a guide for navigating the world. Likewise, this chapter will provide you with a guide for navigating the human body. Instead of north and south, we have cranial (top/head) and caudal (bottom/tail). There are no roads, but many arteries and veins. Our trail markers will be tuberosities and processes. When the tail guide leads you in a certain direction, these termns will help you know which way to go. Before you turn the page, take a moment to "map" your own body. Look down at your toes. Did you notice bow your neck moved? If you wiggle your toes, do they move in the same plane of movement as your neck? Now kick your leg forward. Are you still moving in the same plane? Speaking of wiggling, can you move all of your toes in the same direction? Extend them up to the ceiling. Now flex, or curl, them down. What shape might your joints be to allow this movement? As you are looking down, consider the distance between your heart with hundreds of twists and turns. As you review pages 40-4l, trace the flow of blood from the aorta, down to the capillaries, and back to the vena cava. Where are there parallels between the names of the arteries and veins?

Planes of Movement When the body is in the standard anatomical position, standing erect with tlie palms facing forward (p. 19), it can be divided into three imaginary planes (l.4). These planes help clarify and specify movements. The sagittal plane divides the body into left and right halves. The descriptive terms medial and lateral correlate to the sagittal plane; the actions of flexion and extension occur along this plane. The midline (or midsagittal plane) runs down the center of the body, dividing the sagittal plane in two symmetrical lialves. The frontal (or coronal) plane divides the body into front and back portions. The terns anterior and posterior Transverse relate to the frontal plane; the actions of adrluction and abduction happen along this plane. Dividing the body into upper and lower parts is the transversc plane. Thc terms superior and inferior refer to the Iransverse plane; rotation happens within this planc. Systems of the Body The Skeletal System Cranial portion The bones are linked together to form the skeleton. The skeleton is divided into two sections: the axial and the appendicular skeletons. The axial skeleton is the Skull Facial portion skeleton's center. It includes the cranium, vertebral column, ribs, stermum, and hyoid bone. The appendicular ("appendages") skeleton is composed of the arims and legs, Mandible ineluding the pectoral girdle (scapula and clavicle) and pelvic girdle (hips). The Cardiovascular System Arleries ald veins are the blood vessels of the cardiovaseu- into millions of mieroseupie vessels called eapillaries. lar system. They form an amazing network that transports The walls of the capillaries serve as nutrient and waste blood from the heart, brings it to the body's tissues and exchange sites between tlie body's tissues and the blood. then carries it back to the heart. The Lymphatlc System The lymphatic systen is composed of several organs, yellow fluid called lymph, small microscopic vessels called lymphatics, and lymph nodes. These structures perform many functions throughout the body, such as draining the interstitial fuid that escapes from capillaries and transporting it back to the heart. Lymphatic vessels carry fats from the intestines lo Tansils the blood. Lymphatic tissue also helps the body's immune system defend against foreign cells, microbes, and cancer cells. Traili Along the Edges Shoulder &Arm Spine of the Scapula The spine of the scapula is a superficial ridge located just off the top of the shoulder. It runs at an oblique angle to the body, spanning from the acromion to the medial border. It is all attachment site for the posterior deltoid (p. 67) and middle and lower fibers of the trapezius (p. 68). Scapula - spine 1. Partner prone. Lay your hand across the upper back and slide your fingertips inferiorly until they roll over the superficial spine (2.1+). 2. Strum your fingers vertically, palpating its width and edges. Also explore its entire length by palpating laterally toward the acromion and medially toward the vertebral column. As you strum your fingers over the spine. do you feel a ditch of soft tissue above and below it? ff your partner slowly elevates his scapula, does the spine elevate as well? 2.14 Partner prone. locating the spine of the scapula Because of its central focation. the spine of the scapula if you become lost or confused while palpating the scapula. return to its spine. Medial Border The medial border is the long edge of the scapula that runs parallel to the vertebral column. lt can measure five to seven inches in length, depending on body type. The medial border is an atachment site for the rhomboids (p. 82) and serratus anterior (p. 86) and is deep to the trapezius. Scapula - medial border 1. Partner prone. Place your partner's hand in the small of his back to raise the medial border off the ribs. For more exposure, scoop and raise the shoulder with one hand. 2. Locate the spine of the scapula and glide your finger- tips medially until they slide off thc spine onto the medial border (2.15). 3. Follow the medial border inferiorly and superiorly; note that it extends further inferiorly from the spine of the scapula than superiorly. Does the edge you feel run vertically? 2.15 Sculpting out the medtal border 52 Trail Guide to the Body

As the scapula falls away fron the rib cagc, the superior angle vill be quite palpable. 2.16 Partner prone. pinching the inferior angle The term "winged scapula" refers to a postural the rib cage and visibly protrudes posteriorly. Often indicating a weak serratus anterior muscle, a winged scapula may also involve the muscles that pull the 2.17 Isolating the superior angle shoulder girdle anteriorly. such as the pectoralis major and minor. Actually. a degree of scapular winging normally accurs with scapular abduction. For example, when a boxer throws a punch (and fully abducts the scapula), thc glcnoid fossa is facing anteriorly. In arder for this to happen, the medial border must shift posteriorly away from the rib cage. If this winging of the scapula did not accur, the gleroid fossa would not move anteriorly and the boxer would be flat on the canvas in the first round. 2Shoulder&Arm53

Shoulder & Arm Lateral Border The lateral border extends superiorly and laterally from the inferior angle toward the axilla or "armpit." It is an attachment site for the teres major and teres minor nuscles (pp. 71, 75) and, due to the thickness of these tissues, might not be as clearly defined as the medial border. Scapula - lateral border 1. Prone. Drape the arm off the side of the table. Slide your thumb from the inferior angle superiorly along the lateral border. 2. Fellow the border in the directiou1 of the axilla. If the musculature is too thick to palpate thirough, try curling 2.18 Partner prore. palpating the lateral border your thunb underneath the tissue (2.18). This is mosl effective when locating the infraglenoid tuhercle (see below). Is the edge of bone you are palpating continuous with the inferior angle? As you follow it superiorly. does it lead you in the direction of the axifla? Try the above method with your partner's hand in the small of his back. Infraglenoid Tubercle The infraglenoid tubercle is located at the most supe- rior aspect of the lateral border. The tubercle is not a distinguishable point, but a small spot which serves as an attachment site for the long head of the triceps brachii (p. 97). It lies deep to the teres minor and deltoid muscles. Fxploring the infraglenoid tubercle often clicits tender- 2.19 Partner prone, accessing infraglenoid tubercle ness in the surrounding tissues. By using your broad thumbpad, you will be able to palpate more precisely without causing pain. Scapula - infraglenoid tubercle 1. Prone. Locate the lateral border. 2. Slide along the lateral border to its most superior portion (2.19). To access the landmark directly. you can cither compress through the overlying muscles or curl underneath them. Trail 2 In the Trenches Infraspinous Fossa Shoulder & Arm The scapula contains three fossae, or depressions: the infraspinous, supraspinous, and subscapular. Each fossa is designed to accommodate a muscle belly and its tenndinous attachments. The infraspinous fossa is the triangular area inferior to the spine of the scapula; it is filled with the infraspinaus musele (p. 7+). Scapula - infraspinous fossa 1. Prone. Palpate the spine of the scapula, its medial border and its lateral border to isolate the infraspinous fossa. 2. Cradle the inferior angle in the webbing between your index finger and thumb. Your index finger will rest along the medial border, your thumb along the lateral barder (2.20). 3. Place a finger of the opposite hand along the length. 2.20 Partner prone. isolating of the spine of the scapula. The triangular-shaped area the infraspinous fossa you isolate is the infraspinous fossa. Supraspinous Fossa The supraspinous fossa is located superior to the spine of the scapula. It is small in size, yet quite deep. Because the supraspinatus muscle (p. 7+) attaches to and lies in this basin, the supraspinous fossa is difficult to access directly. Scapula - supraspinous fossa 1. Prone. Drop your thumbpad inferiorly and laterally from the superior angle into the fossa, or lay your 2.21 Partner prone thumb along the spine of the scapula and raise it superiorly into the fossa. 2. Although the fossa is covered by the trapezius and supraspinatus muscles, explore as much as you can cf its size and shape (2.21). 3. Slide your thumbs laterally, noting how the fossa becomes thinner and finally ends at the junction cf the acromion and clavicle. Aetually, the fossa continues underneath the acromion, although it is inaccessible. Are you superior to the spine of the scapula? ff you strum your thumbs verticaly. can you palpate the supraspinatus fibers running horizontally toward the acromion? fossa fos-a L. a shallowr depression 2·Shoulder & Arm 55

text: Shoulder&Arm Subscapular Fossa The subscapular fossa is located on the scapula's anterior (or underside) surface, next to the rib cage. It is the attachment site for the subscapularis (p. 75) and the location of the serratus anterior muscle (p. 86). The fossa can be challenging to access, due to the scapula's close proximity to the rib cage and the nunerous muscle bellies surrounding it. [ Scapula - subscapular fossa 1. Side lying. This position allows tle scapula lo slide away from the rib cage for easier access (2.22). 2. Place your thumb at the middle of the lateral border. Be sure to position your thumb anterior to the large mass of muscles along the lateral border. 3. Slowly sink and curl your thumbpad onto the surface of the fossa. Use your cther hand to maneuver the arm and scapula for a position that best allows your thumb 2.22 Partner side lying, accessing the lateral side of the to sink into the tissue. You may only be able to sink an subscapular fossa. This fossa can be tender when palpated. inch into the fossa. so move slowly and check in with your partner. Can you feer the rib cage and anterior surface of the scapula on either side of your thumb? Try this sarme approach with your partner prone (2.23). Here is a method for palpating the medial portion of the subscapular fossa. The fossa may or may not be accessible, depending on the tissue's flexibility. 1. Side lying. Flex your partner's shoulder and lay your fingertips along the medial border. With the other hand, move the scapula posteriorly (bringing the medial border off the ribs) (2.24). 2. Slowly curl your fingers through the rhomboid and trapezius muscles, under the scapula and onto the fossa. 2.23 Partner prone. palpating the lateral side of the subscapular fossa 2.24 Partner side tying. accessing the medial side of the subscapular fossa 56 Trail Guide to the Body

Trail3 Springboard Ledge Acromion Shoulder & Arm The acromion is the lateral aspect of the spine of the scapula and is located at the top of the shoulder. It has a flat surface and articulates with the clavicle's lateral end. The aeromion serves as an altachment site for the deltoid (p. 67) and trapezius (p. 68) and muscles. The acromial angle is the small corner that can be felt along the acromion's lateral/posterior aspect (see p. 49). ] Acromion 1. Seated or supine. Loeate the spine of tlie scapula. Spine of Acromion 2. Follow the spine as it rises superiorly and laterally to the scapula the top of the shoulder. Use your fingerpads to explore the acromion's flat surface (2.25). 3. Explore and sculpt around all sides of the acromion and its attachment to the clavicle. Is the bone you are palpating superficial and directly on the top of the shoulder? Can you feel the smalf point of the acromial angle on the posterior edge of the acromion? Clavicle The superficial clavicle lies horizontally across the upper chest and has a gentle "S" shape. It is an attachment site for a number of muscles. Both ends of the clavicle are superficial and accessible. The lateral end is relatively flal 2.25 Partrer seated and often rises slightly higher than the acromion. The medial end is round and articulates with the sternum. clavicle 1. Seated. Locate the acromion and walk your fingers medially onto the shaft of the clavicle. 2. Grasp the clavicle's cylindrical body between your Acromion finger and thumb and explore its length froin tlie acromion to the stermum. Observe how its acromial end rises superiorly while its sternal end curves inferiorly (2.26). Have your partner move his shoulder anteriorly. and the shaft of the clavicle will protrude visibty. Can you locate the medial and lateral ends of the clavicle simultaneously? With your fingers at either end of the clavicle, ask your partner to elevate and depress, then adduct and abduct his scapula. As the scapula moves, notice how the ends of the clavicle shift their positions. 2.26 Anterior vlew, clavicle highlighted acromion a-cro-me-onGrk.akron. top + omos, shoulder 2Shoulder & Arm57

Shoulder &Arm Acromioclavicular and Sternoclavicular Joints The acromioclavicular (A/C) joint is the small articulation between the acromion of the scapula and the acromial end of the clavicle. The anterior and superior surfaces of this thin crcvice can be palpated directly. The sternoclavicular (S/C) joint is the articulation between thc sternal end of the clavicle and the sterlum. Unlike the slender, smooth A/C joint, the S/C joint is wedge-shaped and contains a small, impalpable fibrous disc. At rest, only the inferior portion of the sternal end makes contact with the sternum. When the clavicle is elevated, the sternal end pivots on the sternum. A/C joint 1. Seated or supine. Locate the acromion. 2. Glide medially toward the clavicle. Your finger will feel a small "step" as you rise up onto the surfacc of the clavicle. 3. Backtrack slightly. Just lateral to the step will be the A/C joint's slender ditch. 2.27 Accessing Does the acromial end of the clavicle tie slightly higher the A/C joint while than the acromion? Place a finger where you believe the partner elevates and A/C joint to be and ask your partner to slowly elevate and depresses his scapula depress his scapula (2.27). As the scapula rises. do you feel the joint space widen slightly? As it depresses. does the joint space diminish? s/C joint 1. Seated or supine. Slide your fingers medially along thc shaft of the claviele. 2. Just lateral to the body's centerline, the shaft will broaden to become the bulbous stermal end. 3. Locate the S/C joint by sliding your finger medially off the sternal end. Passively elevate, depress, and abduct the scapula. Then cxplorc thc changes occurring at the S/C joint. Place a finger where you believe the S/C joint to be and ask your partner to slowly elevate and depress his scapula (2.28). Can you feel the joint space widen and diminish? 2.28 Palpating the S/C joint while partner elevates and depresses his scapula acromioclavieular 1-0-A--0-2-0-8 sternoclavicular ster-no-kla-vik-u-lar 5.8 3Trail Guide to the Body

Coracoid Process Coracoid process Shoulder & Arm The coracoid process of the scapula is the beak-like projection found inferior to the shaft of the clavicle. Depending on the position of the scapula, it is often found in tlie deliopeetoral groove (p. 62) beiween thc deltoid and pectoralis major fibers. T'he coracoid process can be tender whhen palpated, so proceed carefully. Coracold process 2.29 Anterior view of right shoulder 1. Seated or supine. Lay your thumb along the lateral shaft of the elaviele. 2. Slide inferiorly off the clavicle no more than an inch compressing your fingerpads into the tissue (2.29). 3. As tlie coracoid becomes more apparent, gct a better understanding of its shape and size by seulpting a circle around its edges. Are you inferior to the shaft of the ciavicie? Passively move the scapula with your other hand and feel the coracoid follow your movements. Deltoid Tuberosity Tle deltoid tuberosity is located on the lateral side of the mid-humeral shaft. It is a small, low bump that serves as all attachment site for the converging fibers of the deltoid muscle (p. 67). Deltoid tuberosity 1. Seated or supine. Locate the acromion. 2. Slide off the acromion and down the lateral aspect of the arm (2.30). 3. When you reach the halfway point between the shoulder and clbow, there will be al small mound on the lateral side of the arm. lIf your partner abducts his shoulder. do the deltoid fibers 2.30 Lateral viewaccessing the deltoid tuberosity The coracoid process is an attachment site for several tendons and ligaments. The arrangement of these structures can be illustrated in a clockwise fashion. On the right scapula, the pectoralis minor tendon (a) connects at four o'clock. while the coracobrachialis Coracoin and biceps brachii tendons (b) lie at seven o'clock. The process coracoacromial ligament (c) attaches at ten o'clock and the coracoclavicular ligaments (d) attach further posteriorly on the coracoid process at eleven c'clock and rmidnight. Trail 4 Two Hills and a Valley Shoulder &Arm Greater and Lesser Tubercles, Imtertubercular Groove These three landmarks are located on the proximal humerus deep to the deltoid muscle. The greater tubercle is located inferior and lateral to the acromion. lt is shaped more like a low mound than a pointy hill. The greater tubercle is an attachment site for three of the four rotator cuff muscles —supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres Acromion minor (p. 75). The lesser tubercle is smaller than the greater tubercle and is an attachment site for the fourth rotator cuff muscle -subscapularis (p. 75). Muscles of the Shoulder and Arm Deltoid Shoulder & Arm The triangle-shaped deltoid is located on the cap of the shoulder. The origin of the deltoid (which is, in.terestingly enough, identical to the insertion of the trapezius) curves around the spine of the scapula and clavicle, forming a "v" Posterior Anterior shape. From this broad origin, the fibers converge down the fibers fibers arm to attach at the deltoid tuberosity (2.36). The deltoid fibers can be divided into three segments: Middle the anterior, middle, and posterior fibers. All three groups fibers abduet the humerus, but the anterior ald posterior fibers are antagonists in both flexion/extension and medial/ateral rotation. Trapezius 'T'he trapezius lies superficially along the upper back and neck. Its broad, thin fibers blanket the shoulders, attaching to the occiput (the bone at the base of the head, p. 231), lateral clavicle, scapula, and spinous Superior nuchal line of the occiput processes of the thoracic vertebrae (2.40, 2.42). The trapezius fibers can be divided into three groups: upper (descending) fibers, middle fibers, and lower Upper fibers (ascending) fibers. The upper and lower fibers are antagonists in elevation and depression of the scapula. respectively. All fibers of the trapezius are easy to palpate. Latissimus Dorsi and Teres Major Shoulder & Arm The latissimus dorsi is the broadest muscle of the back. Its thin, superficial fibers originate at the low back, ascend the side of the trunk, and merge into a thick bundle at the axilla (2.+6). Both ends of the latissinus dorsi are difficult to isolate; however, its middle portion next to the lateral border of the scapula is easy to grasp. The teres major is called "lat's little helper" because it is a complete synergist with the latissimus dorsi (2.47). It is superficial and located along the scapula's lateral border between the latissimus dorsi and teres minor. Although they share names, the teres major and teres minor rotate the arm in opposite dircctions—the major mcdially, thc minor laterally. The latissimus dorsi and teres major are sometimes Thoracolumbar called the "handcuff muscles" because their actions fascia collectively bring the arms into the “arresting" position! Rotator Cuff Tendons Shoulder&Arm The tendons of the rotator cuff muscles can be diffieult to access in anatomical position (2.66). The supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons are situated deep to the acromion, while the tendons of the subscapularis and teres minor lie deep to the thick belly of the deltoid. This dilemma can be overcome, however, and the individual tendons isolated by placing the humerus in the Supraspinatus positions outlined below. Since the rotator cuff tendons lie against the surface of the greater or lesser tubercles Infraspinatus of the humerus, they cannot be separated from the underlying bone. Teres minor Supraspinatus tendon 1. The attachment of the tendon will be located just distal Subscapularis to the acromion on the greater tubercle. 2. Its inferior portion is deep to the upper trapezius; however, as the levator ascends the lateral side of the neck, its fibers comc out from under a) Transverse processes of the trapezius and become superficial (2.77). Its belly is C-1 through C-4 approximately two fingers wide with fibers that naturally twist around themselves (2.76). The levator scapula attaches to the transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae (p. 180). Located on the lateral side of the neck, all of these small protuberances extend laterally at approximately the same width, except for the processes of C-1, which are broader. The brachial plexus, a large group of nerves which innervates the arm, exits from the transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae (see p. 27+). When accessing the processcs to locate thie origin of the levator scapula, begin by using your soft fingerpads to avoid compressing a nerve. When exploring the pectoralis major and minor. it is advisable to palpate around breast tissue and not directly into it. Ihis raises the question, "when palpating on a female. how do you access these and other chest muscles without contacting breast tissue?" The most important aspect when palpating near breast tissue is communicating your intentions to your partner. Also. encourage her to let you know if at any time she wishes to stop. Assuming your partner is draped with a sheet or wearing a sports bra. the key to safe and comfortable palpation around breast tissue is positioning your Supine client so the breast tissue will naturally shift away from where you are accessing. For instance, in a supine position (above). the breast will shift laterally. allowing easier access to the sternal and upper pectoral regions. In this position. however, larger breasts may crowd the axillary region. In such situations you could either ask your partner to shift and hold her breast medially. allowing you to access the axilla, or use the back of your own hand to push the tissue medially. In a side lying position (left), the breast wil fall medially. opening up the axillary region. The axilla can be opened up further by passively shifting the shoulder anteriorly. 90Trail Guide to the Body

text: Shoulder & Arm 1. Side lying. Supporting your parther's arm, flex the shoulder and pull it anteriorly toward you. This position not only brings the pectoralis major off the chest wall, but also allows the breast tissue to fall away from the area you are palpating. 2. Grasping the pectoralis major, explore its mass from the ribs to the humerus (2.91). Passively flex and extend the shoulder, perceiving the changes in the tension of the tissues. 2.91 Partner side lying Here is a way to get a sense of the antagonistic movements of thc pectoralis major's uppcr and lowcr fibcrs. 1. Supine. Begin with your partner's hand raised up toward the ceiling. As you create resistance, ask your partner to flex his shoulder. "Meeting my resistance, try to bring your hand over your head." The upper fibers will contract while the lower fibers remain lax. 2. Ask him to extend against your resistance. "Now try to bring your hand toward your hips." Here the lower fibers will contract while the upper fibers relax (2.92). Shoulder& Arm Pectoralis Minor The pectoralis minor lies next to the rib cage deep to the pectoralis major (2.93). Its fibers run perpendicular to the pectoralis major fibers from the scapula's cora- coid process to the upper ribs. During aerobie activity the pectoralis minor helps to elevate the rib cage for inhalatiou. The major vessels serving the arm- the brachial plexus, axillary artery and vein—cross underneath the pectoralis minor, creating the potential for neurovascular compression by this muscle (2.95). Aecess to the pectoralis minor can be achieved by either pressing through or sliding underneath the thick pectoralis major. The second method is more specific and will be outlined here. The pectoralis minor call be sensitive, so palpate slowly, allowing your fingers or thumb to sink into the tissue. Shoulder &Arm Clavicle Subclavius As its name suggests, the subclavius is located undermeath the clavicle. Its fibers run parallel to the clavicle, deep to the pectoralis major, and can be challenging to truly isolate (2.98). On quadrupeds (four-legged animals), the subclavius is quite large and plays an important role in stabilizing the clavicle and shoulder girdle during locomotion. A human's subclavius, on the other hand, is a small, secondary muscle. Subclavius A Depress the clavicle and draw it anteriorly Elevate the first rib (to assist during inhalation) 2.98 Anterior view of subclavius Stabilize the sternoclavicular joint First rib and cartilage Inferior surface of middle one-third of clavicle N Subclavian C5. 6 Subclavlus 1. Side lying. Support the arm in a flexed position and pull it anteriorly. This position brings the clavicle and pectoralis major off the rib cage and allows your thumb to curl even further around the clavicle. 2. Place your thumb and fingers at the center of the clavicle. Slowly curl your thumb around the clavicle's underside, trying to access the subclavius (2.99). You might not access a muscle belly, but might feel instead some slightly dense tissue tucked under the clavicle. 3. Try this method with your partner in a supine position. 2.99 Partner side lying with arm flexed Can you detect a sfender strip of tissue deep to the clavicle? Coracobrachialis Shoulder&Aim Coracoid The coracobrachialis is a small, tubular muscle located process in the axilla (2.109). Sometimes known as the "armpit muscle, it is a secondary flexor and adductor of the shoulder. In anatomical position, the coracobrachialis is deep to the pectoralis major and anterior deltoid and lies anterior to the axillary artery and brarhial plexus. Abducting the shoulder (opening up the axilla) brings the belly of the coracobrachialis to a superficial and palpable position. Shoulder &Arm Trapezoid Coracoclavicular Ligament The coracoclavicular ligament is com posed of two smaller ligaments: the trapezoid and conoid. Both ligaments streteh fron the coracoid process of the scapula to the inferior surface of the clavicle (2.115, p. 102). Together they provide stability for the acromioclavicular joint and form a strong bridge between the scapula and clavicle. The coracoclavicular ligament can be accessed by palpaling between the clavicle and coracoid process or curling under the anterior aspect of the clavicle. Coracoclavicular ligament 1. Seated or supine. Abduct and medially rotate the shoulder. This position brings the ligaments more to the surface. 2.119 Anterior view of right shoulder. 2. Locate the coracoid process of the scapula and the palpating coracociavicular ligament shaft of the clavicle. 3. Palpate in the space between these landmarks. Roll your thumbpad across its fibers (2.119). Unlike the superficial pectoralis major fibers, the ligaments will feel like solid, taut bands. Passively move the shouider girdle in several directions and see if a particular position allows you greater access to the ligarments. Coracoacromial Ligament Unlike most ligaments that hold two bones together, the coracoacromial ligament attaches the scapula's coracoid proccss to its acromion (2.1z0). Along with the acromion, tlhis ligaiment forms the coracoacromial arch across tlie top of the shoulder. This arch helps to protect the rotator cuff tendons and subacromial bursa froin direct trauma 2.120 Anterior view, palpating by the acromion. The wide band of the coracoacromial the coracoacromlal ligament ligament lies deep to the deltoid but is still accessible. Acromion Coracoacromial ligament Coracoid process 1. Supine or seated. Locate the coracoid process. Then locate the anterior edge of the acromicn. 2. Palpating deep to the deltoid fibers, explore between these landmarks for the wide band of the coracoacromial ligament. Strum your finger aeross its fibers (2.120). 3. To bring the ligament closer to the surface, try extending the arm. This position will roll the humeral head anteriorly and press the ligament forward. Are you between the acromion and the coracoid process? Place one finger on the ligament and passively move the shourder girdle in various positions. Can you feel how the ligament's relationship to the surrounding tissues changes as the position of the shouider changes? 104 Trail Guide to the Body

Subacromial Bursa Subacromial bursa Shoulder & Arm Also known as the subdeltoid bursa, this sizable fluid sac Deltoid (cut) Subscapularis has two major sections (2.12l). The lateral portion creates a smooth surface for the acromion and deltoid to glide over the lhead of the humerus and rotator cuff tendons. Tlhe medial part cushions the coracoacromial ligament from the supraspinatus tendon. With the arm at the side, most of the bursa is. underneath the acromion and inaccessible. Extending the shoulder joint, however, will bring the bursa forward. Since abduction of the shoulder compresses the bursa, can be used as an indicator of subacromial bursitis. Subacromial bursa 1. With your partner seated, stand behind him and 2.121 Anterior view of right shoulder locate the acromion. 2. Drop your fingers off the anterior edge of the acroinion. Then, with your other hand, slowly extend Acromion Bursa the shoulder by pulling tlhe elbow posteriorly; this will bring the bursa out from under the acromion. You will be palpating at the depth between the deltoid and rotator cuff tendons (2.122). 3. A little hint—- palpate gently. Bursae are delicate 2.122 Lateral view with right structures and best accessed with a soft touch. If it is shoulder extended. palpating inflamed it will be acutely tender. the subacromial bursa Axillary Lymph Nodes The axillary lymph nodes are located in the axilla. When palpating in the axillary rcgion, usc a deliberate yet gentle touch to avoid tickling your partner. Also, move slowly, using gentle pressure to avoid impinging the artery and nerves. ] Axillary lymph nodes 1. Supine or seated. Abduct the arm and slowly sink two fingers up into the axilla. 'Then bring the arim back to the side of the body to soften the axillary tissue further. 2. Slide your fingers up to the top of the axilla and then medially toward the rib cage. Often there will be a few Iymph nodes located against the ribs (2.123). 3. Move to the lateral side of thc axilla and use light pressure against the humerus to locate the strong pulse Cf the brachial artery. The vessel will be positioned between the stringy coracobrachialis and long head of the triceps brachii muscle. 2.123 Anterior view of right shoulder. accessing the nodes 2Shoulder & Arm 105

Shoulder &Arm Brachial Artery Biceps brachii The brachial artery is a continuation of the axillary artery and runs between the biceps and triceps brachii. Its pulse can be felt between these muscles on the medial side of the arm (see p. 101). Before the brachial artery braches off to the radial and ulnar arteries, its pulse can be felt at the elbow, just medial to the biceps brachii tendon. Brachial artery 1. Supine or seated. Abduct the arm and place your fingerpads on thie medial side of the arm. A helpful guide is the shallow dip which forms between the Triceps brachii biceps and triceps (2.12+). 2. Gently press your fingers toward the shaft of the humerus to feel the brachial pulse. 3. The brachial pulse canl also be detected just medial to the distal tendon of the biceps brachii. 2.124 Anterior/medial view. palpating the brachial pulse between the biceps and triceps brachii On to fhe forearm and hand, where we'l find flexors, extensors, and an "anatomical snuffbox." 106 Trail Guide to the Body

Forearm & Hand 3 The bunman band permeates may aspects of culture, including our language. You can hope to gain the upper hand when the left hand doesn't kncw what the right hamd's doing. You can lend a hand if things get out of hand. And if all goes well, it's a snap of the fingers. cracking an egg without our hands. Incredibly, the majority of muscles that provide your fngers with strength and versatility are located nowhere near your digits, but in the forearm. The fingers contain only the attaching tendons. Consider the following questione as you explore: With a tennis ball (or similarly-sized object) in your hand, set your wrist in a neutral position and give the ball a good squeeze. Then squeeze with your wrist flexed. Finally, try an extended position. How did the 1.1 strength of your grip change in each of the positions? Why do you think that is? Now place your right hand on a table and slowly, but dramatically, move your fingers as if you're playing a massive piano. Use your left hand to explore the forearm (and especially the wrist) to feel all of the contractions of various muscles. Follow a muscle or tendon to the elbow or past the wrist.

Trail1 Knob Hill Olecranon Process and Fossa The clecranon process (or elbow) is located on the proximal end of the ulna and articulates with tlhe distal humerus. Its large surface is the attachment site for the triceps brachii muscle. It forms the “point" of tlhe elbow and is easily located. The olecranon fossa is a large cavity on the posterior, distal end of the humerus designed to accommodate the Forearm & Hand 3.12 Palpating the olecranon process when the elbow is extended. Epicondyles of the Humerus As the humerus extends down the arm, its distal end broadens medially and laterally. Directly medial from the olecranon process is the medial epicondyle. Trail 4 Walking on Your Hands Carpals There are eight carpal bones located at the wrist. Small and uniquely shaped, the carpals are closely wedged Scaphoid Lunate Triquetrum together between the distal radius and ulna and the metacarpals. The carpals form two rows, each coinposed of faur banes (3.27). Located distal to the flexor crease at the wrist, under thhe heel of the hand, the carpals lie deep to numerous flexor and extensor tendons. These overlying tendons, combined Scaphoid Forearmi & Hand tubercle with the carpals' compact arrangement, make isolating Pisiform individual bones a challenge. The next few pages present the carpals in pairs. We will Trapezitm tubercle bcgin by exploring the carpals as one large group. We will thhen access the pisiform, triquetrum, and the hamate, as these are possibly the easiest to isolate. Scaphoid Scaphoid The peanut-shaped scaphoid (or navicular) is the most commonly fractured carpal. It is located on the radial side of the hand, distal to the styloid process of the radius. Although the scaphoid forms the floor of the tendinous "anatomical snuffbox" (p. 153), it is still accessible from the dorsal, palmar, and radial sides of the wrist. scaphold Forearmi & Hand 3.37 Dorsal view of right 1. Beginning on the wrist's radial surface, locate the hand. wrist in neutral radius's styloid process. Slide your thumb distally off the process, falling between the superficial tendons and into the natural diteh where the scaphoid will be found (3.37). 2. Maintain your position and passively adduct the wrist. As you do so, feel for the scaphoid to bulge into your thumb (3.38). Now abduct the wrist and feel how the scaphcid disappears back into the wrist. 3. From here, explore the scaphoid's dorsal and palmar surfaces. On the palnar surface, along the flexor crease, is the scaphoid tuberele (p. 12+). Are you distal to the end of the styloid process of the 3.38 Dorsal view of right radius? During adduction and abduction. do you feel the hand, wrist adducted scaphoid protrude and then disappear? Trapezium Located distal to the scaphoid, the sinall trapeziuin articulates with the base of the first metacarpal. This articulation, the first carpomectacarpal joint, is the source of tlie thumb's unique movements. The trapeziuin is most accessible on its radial or dorsal side and can be isolated either distally from the scaphoid or proximally from the first metacarpal. Trapezlum 1. Palpating along the hand's radial/dorsal side, locate the scaphoid. Then slide distally (3.39). Scaphoid 2. You might. by aecident, pass tlhe trapezium and ride Up onto the base of the first metacarpal. If so, simply slide back proximally to the trapezium. To check if you are indeed feeling the base of the first metacarpal instead of the trapezium. ask your partner to sfowly flex and extend his thumb. With this action, the base of the first metacarpal should clearly move. To check for the trapezium: Are you distal to the scaphoid and proximal to the base of the first metacarpal? Lunate and Capitate The lunate is the most frequently dislocated carpal. Located distal and medial to Lister's tubercle (p. I 18), Lister's tubercle it is relatively inaccessible when the wrist is in a neutral position; flexing the wrist, however, will slide the lunate to the dorsal surface. The capitate is the largest of the carpals and is located distal to the lunate. It has a shallow diteh on its dorsal Lunate surface that can be easily palpated. Forearun & Hand Although the lunate and capitate lie deep to the extensor tendons, both carpals are accessible on theit Capitate dorsal surfaces and can be isolated between Lister's tubercle and the shaft of the third mctacarpal (3.43). Overview: Muscles of the Forearm and Hand Tlhe muscles of thie forcarm primarily create movement at the wrist and fingers. Mlany have sinall, fusiform bellies tlhat connect lo space-cfficient tendons in the lower forearm. These tendons extend distally into the wrist, hand, and fingers. The crowded muscle bellies Biceps brachii Pronator teres and tendons of the forearm can be challenging to isolate. To sim plify naiters, the museles of this chapier have been arranged into four prinary groups: Brachialis Forearmi & Hand a. Nluscles that act prinarily at the elbow: Brachialis Flexor carpi radialis Brachioradialis Bicipital b. Mluscles that move tlhie wrist and/or fingers srsonauade (carpi, digitonum, or palmaris muscles). This group can be further subdivided into four smaller groups: Extensors of the wrist and fingers Palmaris longus Flexors of the wrist and fingers Brachioradialis Adductors of the wrist Abductors of tlie wrist (Some muscles that act upon the wrist can actually Flexor carpi ulnaris move it in two directions. Flexor carpi ulnaris, for example, both flexes and adducts the wrist.) c. Aluscles that crealc tlne pivoting action behween the radius and ulna: Pronator teres Flexor digitorum Pronator quadratus Flexor superficialis Supinator pollicis snbuo d. Short and long muscles that mancuver tlhc thumb (pollicis tmuscles). Antebrachial fascia Palmar aponeurosis Distinguishing between abduction (left) and adduction (right) of the wrist can be confusing if the forearm is pronated or supinated. For instance, if you pronate your forearm (palm toward the floor) and then 2dduct at the wrist. your hand will move 2way from the centerline of your body and it will appear as though you have abducted your wrist. Brachioradialis Distinguishing between the Flexor and Extensor Groups of the Forearm Before we isolate specific flexors and extensors, let us Flexors first determine the location of these twe muscle groups. The flexors and extensors of the hand and wrist are located in the forearm. In anatomical position, the Forearm & Hand flexors are located on the anterior/medial (hairless) side of thie forearm, while the extensors are positiened on tlie posterior/lateral (hairy) side. Shaft of the ulna The brachioradialis and shaft of the ulna can be used as clear dividing lines bctwccn thcse muscle groups Extensors (3.63). Both of these structures run superficially dowll the opposite sides of the forearm, separating the flexors alnd extennsors. 3.63 Cross section of the right forearm, diagonal line dividing the flexors and extensors Flexors and extensors 1. Shake hands with your partner and flex the elbow to 90*. Locate the brachioradialis and shaft of the ulna (p. ll6). Palpatc thc length of thcsc structurcs, observing how they divide the forearin into two halves. Brachioradialis 2. Move medially from the shaft of the ulna onto the flexors of the forearm. Explore this half of thie forearm, noting the girth of these muscles. 3. Ask your partner to slightly flex her wrist against your resistance (3.64). Note the contraction. of the flexors. 4. Mlove to the lateral side cf the shaft of thhe ulna alnd explore the extensor bellies (3.65). Notice how they are smaller and niore sinewy than the flexor bellies. Ask your partner to extend her wrist against your resistance, feeling the extensors contract. When your partner curis (ffexes) her wrist. do the muscles on the hairless side of the forearm contract? Do the extensors contract when the hand moves in the opposite direction (extension)? 3.64 Anterior/medial view of right forearm, highlighting flexors Brachioradialis 3.65 Lateral view of right forearm.highlighting extensors 134 Trail Guide to the Body

Extensors of the Wrist and Fingers Extensor Carpi Radialis Longus Extensor Carpi Rudialis Brevis Extensor Carpi Ulnaris Extensor carpi Extensor Digitorum radialis longus The four extensors create extension primarily at the wrist and fingers. They are situated between the brachioradialis Extensor Extensor carpi and the shaft of the ulna along the forearm's lateral, radialis brevis Forearln & Hand posterior surface. All of these muscles are superficial and ulnaris accessible, though challenging to truly isolate. Anconeus Olecranon process The anconeus is a weak elbow extensor located lateral Lateral epicondyle to the olecranon process. Triangular-shaped, it originates at the lateral epicondyle of the humerus and fans out to attach on the shaft of the ulna (3.78). The ancoeus is superficial, yet can be difficult to differentiate from the surroun.ding extensors. Pronator Teres Iocated on the anterior surface of the forearm, the round pronator teres is tucked between the brachioradialis and forearm flexors (p. I+0). It is partially superficial and the only muscle in this vicinity with oblique fibers (3.99). The pronator teres is an antagonist to the biceps brachii and supinator (*carrying a bowl of soup") muscles and creates pronation of the forearm ("prone to spill it"). The Forearm & Hand distal tendon of biceps brachii, situated just lateral to thie pronator teres, is a good landmark for locating its fibers. 3.100 0rigin Pronator Teres and Insertion A Pronate the forearm (radioulnar joints) Assist to flex the elbow (humeroulnar joint) 3.99 Anterior view of right Common flexor tendon from medial epicondyle of humerus and forearm, showing pronator teres coronoid process of the ulna Middle of lateral surface of the radius Biceps brachii N Median C6. 7 tendon Pronator teres 1. The majority of the muscle is inaccessible, except for its most lateral portion. Muscles of the Thumb and Hand Long Muscles of the Thumb The bellies of abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis longus and brevis lie along the posterior aspect of the forearm, deep to the wrist extensors (3.115-3.117). Their distal tendons, however, are superficial and form the "anatomical snufbox." Used historically as a platform for inhaling a variety of substances, this small cavity is located along the dorsal surface of the hand, just distal to the styloid process of the radius. Forearr & Hand The belly of the flexor pollicis longus lies on the forearm's anterior surfare, deep to tlie wrist flexors, and is inaecessible. Its long, distal tendon travels thirough the carpal tunnel between the thenar eminence muscles to Abductor pollicis longus the distal phalanx of the thumb (3.120). text: Spine & Thorax 4 The spine and thorax play a vital role not only in movement, but also in1 Topographical Views 168 sustaining life. Your vertebral column supports the torso while protecting Exploring the Skin and Fascia 169 the delicate spinal cord. Your rib cage, held together with interweaving Bones cf the Spine and Thorax 170 muscles, shields many of the vital organs and is key to respiration. Your Bony Landmarks of the Spine abdomen is walled off by several thin layers of muscle that support speech, and Thorax 171 laughter, and the digestion of your lunch. Overview: Bony Landmark Trails 174 Because these structures work around the clock in all positions, they are Overview: Muscles of the Spine also prone to injury. The incidence of neck and back pain is high, so kcep and Thcrax 188 tlhis in mind as you explore tlhe structures in this chapter. Synergists-Muscles Working Together 194 Cousider the following questions as you explore: Muscles of the Spine and Thorax 196 ● Place both of your hands around your rib cage and take deep, slow Ligaments and Other Structures breaths. In which directions are your ribs moving? Next, stand in front of the Spine and Thorax 218 of a inirror and take in a big breath. What muscles might be helping? T'hen, forcefully exhale as though you are blowing out the candles on. your birthday cake. What museles do you think might be helping now? With your liands beside your navel, do an cver-so-slight sit up. Relax, take a breath and do it again. Note how the other muscles of your torso, neck, and shoulder also engage with this movement. It's not just your abdominals working, is it? 167

Trail1 Midline Ridge Spinous Processes of the Vertebrae A spinous process is a vertebra's posterior projection. As a group, the spinous processes form the visible row of bunps that run down the center of the back. They are designed as attachment sites for layers of muscles, ligaments and fasciae. The lumbar, thoracic, and cervical spinous processes differ from one another in several respects. The spinous processes of the lumbar vertebrae are much larger than tlie thoracie cr cervical processes. 'Tall and stocky, the tips of the lumbar processes may feel more like short 4.24 Lateral/posterior view. strips than points. The bodies of these vertebrae are quite spine in neutral position massive and tall; they may have a finger's width of spacc between their processes. 'The thoracic spinous processes are smaller and closer together than their lumbar counterparts and angle downward. The cervical spinous processes are shorter and smaller Spine & Thorax than the thoracic processes. Because of the lordotic curve in the cervical spine and the overlying ligamentum nuchae, the cervical spinous processes are actually dee pcr than the thoracic and lumbar vertebrae. The first cervical vertebra (C-l), or atlas as it is called, is the only vertebra that does not have a spinous process. Spinous processes of the vertebrae 1. Partner seated with trunk and neck slightly flexed (this will streteh the overlying tissues and allow the processes to move posteriorly for easier access). Place your fingers along the midline of the back and locate the long line of processes (4.2+, 4.25). 2. Slide your fingers slowly up and down the spine, palpating the different sizes, prominences, and spaces between processes. Some processes might present themselves immediately, while olhers might be more difficult to find. Ask your partner to slowly flex and extend his spine, noting the movement of the processes. 3. Try this same method with your partner prone. Can yov sculpt out the sides of the processes as you palpate them? is there a dip superior and inferior to the point you feer? Can you line up three fingers on a series of processes or in the spaces between the processes? Partuer prone or seated. Palpate the entire spinal coluimn and count the spinous processes. How many can you feel? All the vertebrae (except C-l) have spinous processes, making a total of twenty-three. Use the intersecting spinous processes, sucli as C-7, T-12, and L-+, to check your aceuracy. 4.2S Posterior/lateral view. paipating the lumbar spinous processes 176 Trail Guide to the Body

Because everyone's body is unique, these intersecting landmarks are not definitive; they are best used as guides. L-4 and top of the iliac crest 1. The twelth rib has a slender, spearlike shape and angles inferiorly. It may vary in length between three to six inches and can be used as a locator for the spinous process of T-12. The strategy is to locate the tip of the twelfth rib and follow its shaft to the spinous process. With the tip isolated, gently follow the shaft of the rib medially, noting how it lies at an angle. As the rib lies deep to the crector spinae muscles, you might losc contact with its most medial portion. Transverse Processes of the Cervicals The transverse processes (TVPs) of the cervical vertebrae are located on the side of the neck. Old Hollywood films put the Frankenstein monster's neck bolts into his TVPs! The TVPs extend inferiorly from the mastoid process and many are deep to the sternocleidomastoid muscle (p. 2++). All of the TVPs are the sane widtl except for 4.33 The arrangement of the the TVPs of C-1, which are much wider. cervical TvPs simulate that of a long. dangling earring The TVPs of C-l are located just distal and anterior to the tip of the mastoid process (p. 233) and are relatively accessible. All of the TVPs serve as attachment sites for various nuscles, including the scalenes and levator scapulae. T'he brachial plexus, a large group of nerves that innervates the arm, exits between the TVPs. When Spine & Thorax first accessing the TVPs, use the flat of your thumb or fingerpads. As your palpation skills improve, explore the TVPs' surfaces more specifically. TvPs of cervicals 1. Partner supine. Place your fingers on the side of the neck below the earlobes. 2. Using your flat thumbpads, slide anteriorly and Dosteriorly to feel the ridge of TVPs. Explore the length of the neck (4.3+). 3. Lamina Groove of the Thoracic and Lumbar Vertebrae The lamina groove of the thoracic and lumbar vertebrae is located between their spinous and transverse processes. Shaped like a long, vertical trough, the lamina groove oul umop sossoreoid i se qipim pun ydop ui spuedxo spine. In the thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, the lamina groove is filled with the layers of the erector spinac and transversospinalis muscles. Because of this overlying tissue, the lamina groove is difficult to access directly,. but its borders (the spinous and transverse processes) are palpable. Lamina groove of the thoracic and lumbar vertebrae 4.43 Lateral/superior view, accessing the lamina groove of the 1. Partncr prone. Locate the spinous proccsses of the thoracic vertebrae. Using firm pressure, explore between these landmarks in the lamina groove (4.43). Note the thick inuscle tissue that lies in this groove. 3. Try this same method in the lumbar region (4.44). Observe how the lamina groove widens and deepens and how the muscle tissue is thicker in this region compared with the muscle tissue in the thoracic region. text: Thoracic vertebrae First Rib Clavicle First rib Unlike its cohorts, the first rilb is difficult to isolate along the anterior thorax. It lies directly beneath the clavicle and then quickly curves toward the back (4.47). It can, Second rib however, be accessed in the posterior triangle (p. 226) of the neck, which is formed by the clavicle and the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles. The scalene imuscles (p. 246) fan across the posterior triangle and attach to the first and second ribs. To access the first rib, you must palpate through the scalenes. The brachial plexus (p. 274) and subclavian artery pass between the first rib and the clavicle. First rlb 4.47 Superior view of thorax 1. Partner supine. Soften the overlying tissue by passively elevating your partner's shoulder. Spine & Thorax 2.1 Locate the clavicle and upper fap of the trapezius to identify the posterior triangle. Place your thumbpad between these structures. 3.5 Slowly sink into the tissue of the scalene muscles, directing your fingers straight in an inferior direction toward your partner's feet (4.48). As your fingers sink into the tissue, you will meet the solid resistance of the Compression or impingement or the brachia! shaft of the first rib. plexus or one of its nerves can create a sharp shooting sensation down the arm. ff this shouid occur, adjust your position to one side. Also. ask Ask your partner to take a slow. deep breath into the your partner for feedback. upper chest. Can you feer the rib rise? Partner supine. When palpating in the posterior triangle of the neck, the posterior aspect of the first rib can sometimes be confused with the superior angle of the scapula. Distinguish between these structures by palpating in the posterior triangle and locating what you believe to be the first rib. "Check it" by passively elevating and depressing the scapula. The first rib should remain stationary during tlhis movenent. It is not uncommor far the number of ribs to vary from person to person. There are ordinarily twelve pairs of ribs. but on some people eleven or thirteen pairs can be fcund. If there is an extra rib. it may be bilateral or merely unilateral and will be found either in the cervical or lumbar areas. text: Ideally, the ribs are designed to expand in three directions during inhalation: anterior/posterior. lateral, and superior. Yet. for reasons ranging from posture to emotional trauma. few people truly breathe in this manner. Often the breath becomes restricted to a portion of the thorax, and the ribs will move in only one or two directions. 1. With this in mind. ask your partner to stand and breathe normally. Observe any changes in the shape or move- ment of the thorax, shoulders, and abdomen. Positiar or rhe rcs c.nng inhaiation 2. Then lay your hands on all sides of the rib cage and feel for activity in the thorax. Do the ribs move in all three directions? Do some move individually? 3. Ask your partncr to inhale dceply and exhale fully. Spine& Thorax Explore the ribs and anterior neck muscles (scalenes and sternocleidomastoid) during inhalation. These muscles will tighten to elevate the upper ribs. Try these exercises with your partner seated. prone. and supine. Posithon af the!ts during exhalation Eleventh and Twelfth Ribs 'The eleventh and twelfth ribs are called "floating ribs" because they do not attach to the costal cartilage. Both ribs have a sleiider, spearlike shape and lie roughly at a 4§ angle. Their medial portions lie deep to the thick erector spinae muscles; however, their lateral aspects and tips are palpable. The eleventh rib is six to eight inches in length and extends halfway around the body. 'I'he twelfth rib can vary in length from three to six inches. Since anomalies are common in either the length or number of ribs, your partner's ribs might not match this description. (See p. 177 for more information about finding the twelfth rib.) Eleventh and twelfth ribs 1. Prone. Reaching across to the body's opposite side, place your hand along the lateral portiou of the ribs. 2. Slide inferiorly to the bottom of the rib cage, allowing your hand to sink into the soft abdominal tissue. Compressing your fingerpads into the side of the thorax, explore this region for the tips of the eleventh Eleventh and and twclfth ribs (4.49). twelfth ribs 3. With the tips isolated, gently follow the shafts of the ribs medially, noting how they run at an angle. Can you feel two tips. one of which is more laterai than the other? Ask your partner to take a slow. deep breath 4.49 Partner prone and note whether the tips or the bodies of the ribs press into your hand. 4Spine & Thorax187

Muscles of the Spine and Thorax Erector Spinae Group Spinalis Longissimus Iliocostalis The erector spinae group runs from the sacrum to the occiput along the posterior aspcct of the vertebral column. Its musculature has a dense, layered arrangement that can be diffieult to visualize. It might sim plify matters if you iniagine the erector spinae muscles as a tall poplar tree (+.60) with three main branches- the spinalis, C-7 longissimus, and iliocostalis (4.61). These branches can then be subdivided into numerous smaller branches such as spinalis thoracis, longissinus capitis, iliocostalis lumborum, and more. The spinalis is the smallest of the three muscles and lies elosest to the spine in the lamina groove (4.62). The Spine & Thorax thick longissimus and lateral iliocostalis form a visible mound alongside the lumbar and thoracic spine (4.64, 4.65). The long tendons of iliocostalis extend laterally beneath the scapula. In the lumbar region, the erectors lie deep to the thin but dense thoracolumbar fascia (p. 220). In the thoracic and cervical areas, they are deep to the trapezius, the rhomboids, and the serratus posterior superior and inferior. As a group, the erectors are easily palpated along Spinalis the entire length of the back and neck; locating a specific branch of the erectors, however, can be challenging. Erector spinae group 1. Partner prone. Lay both hands along either side of the lumbar vertebrae. Locate the region of the lower erectors by asking your partner to alternately raise and lower his feet slightly. The erectors do not, of course, raise the feet, but they will contract in order to stabilize the pelvis. Notice how the strong, rounded crector fibers tighten and relax with this action (4.68). 2. As your partner maintains this contraction, palpate inferiorly onto the sacrum and then superiorly along the thoracic vertebrae. Ask your partner to extend his spine and neck slightly in order to contract the erectors in the thoracic region (4.69). 3. Follow the ropy fibers of the erectors between the scapulae and along the back of the neck. These fibers are smallest in the cervical region and are prinarily siluated lateral to the lamina groove. Splne& Thorax 4. With your partner relaxed, sink your fingers into the crector fibers, feeling their ropy texture and vertical direction (4.70). Is the tissue you are palpating directly beside the spinous 4.68 Partner prone. palpating the lower processes of the vertebrae? Do the fibers run parallel to erectors while your partner raises his feet the spine? When the muscles are contracted. can you locate the lateral edge of the erector group? Can you distinguish the fiber direction of the middle trapezius. rhomboids. and erectors between the scapulae? 4.69 Partner prone. palpating the upper erectors whileyourpartnerextendshis spine 4.70 Partner prone. strumming your thumbs across the fibers of the spinalis 4  Spine & Thorax199

Transversospinalis Group Superior Multifidi nuchal line Rotatores Senispinalis Capitis Deep to the erector spinae muscle group is the Semispinalis capitis transversospinalis muscle group. The transversospinalis (cut and reflected) is composed of three branches-nultifidi, rotatores, and semispinalis-and extends the length of the vertebral column. Unlike the long, vertical erector fibers, the branches of the transversospinalis consist of many short, diagonal fibers. These fibers form an intricate stitchlike Rotatores design that links the vertebrae togcther. The namc "transversospinalis" refers to the fact that the fibers of this muscle group extend at varying lengths from the transverse and spinous processes of the vertebrae. Multifidi The surprisingly thick multifidi are directly accessible Spine & Thorax in the lumbar spine. They are the only muscles with fibers that lie across the posterior surface of the sacrum. The shorter, smaller rotatores lie deep to the multifidi 4.71 Posterior view (4.72). The semispinalis capitis is located along the thoracic and cervical vertebrae and ultimately reaclies the cranium (4.71). Both semispinalis muscles form the twin "speed bumps" easily seen on the posterior neck when the neck is extended against resistance. It can be difficult to isolate the individual bellics of the transversospinalis muscles as they are closely interwoven; however, as a group, their mass or density can be easily felt along the lamina groove of the thoracic and lumbar vertebrae. Quadratus Lumborum Erector spinae group Quadratus lumborum Although it would seem to be the deepest nnuiscle of the low back, the quadratus lumborum is, strangely enough, the deepest muscle of the abdomen (+.92). Stretching from the posterior ilium to the transverse processes of the lumbar vertebrae and twelfth rib, this squat muscle is simply an abdaminal muscle located on the posterior surface of the thorax. While the medial portion of the quadratus lumborum is buried beneath the thoracolumbar fascia and the thick erector spinae (t.9l), its lateral edge is accessible from the side of the torso. Using slow, firm pressure, sink your thumb medially toward the lumbar vertebrae and into the edge of the quadratus (4.95). 4. Diaphragm T'he diaphragm is the prinary nuscle of respiration and is unique in botlh its design and function. Its broad, umbrellalike shape separates the upper and lower thoracic cavities (4.10s). Tlhe diaphragim's muscle fibers attach to the inner surface of the ribs and the lumbar vertebrae and converge at the central tendon (4.109). The diaphragin creates inspiration (inhalation) when its muscle fibers contract and pull the central tendon inferiorly. Because tlie central tendonl is altached lo tlhe connective tissue that surrounds the lungs, a vacuum is Central tendon created in the upper thoracic cavity, pulling air into the Jungs. On cxhalation, thc muscle fibers of the diaphragm relax, releasing thie central tendon and allowiuig the lungs to deflate Diaphragm Althiough only a small portion of the diaphragm is accessible, the muscle's effect on the thorax and breathing Splne & Thorax is easily felt. Diaphragm Draw down the central tendon of the diaphragm A Increase the volume of the thoracic cavity during inhalation Costal attachment: Inner surface of lower six ribs 4.108 Lateral view o1 thorax. showing Lumbar attachment: Upper two or three lumbar vertebrae diaphragm in position of exhalation Sterna! attachiment: Inner part of xiphoid process Central tendon An involuntary contraction of the Phrenic C3. 4. 5 diaphragm will cause air to rush into N the lungs and the vocal cords to snap shut. The audible result is a hiccup.
