Intercostals Better known to carmivores as the neat on spare ribs, the intercostals are the small, slender muscles betwcen the ribs. They are divided into two groups: the external External and the internal intercostals (+.112). The fibers of thesc intercostals two groups run perpendicular to each other and can bc visualized as extensions of the external and internal oblique muscles (pp. 210, 211). The intercostals help to stabilize the rib cage and assist in respiration, but their specific role is debatable. Although the entire rib cage lies deep to one or more layers of imuscle, portions of the intercostals remain easily accessible. It is not possible, howevcr, to distinguish the 4.112 Anterior external intercostals from the internal intercostals. view of rib cage Since the ribs and the spaces between them can be sensitive areas to access, use slow, firm hand movements. text: Intertransversarii As their name suggests, these small, short muscles span between the transverse processes. They are thie deepest muscles in the cervical and lumbar regions and, for this. reason, are nearly impossible to detect, let alone access (+.117). Intertransversarii A Unilaterally: Laterally flex the vertebral column to the same side Bifaterally: Extend the vertebral column Cervical: Spanning the transverse processes of vertebrae C-2 to C-7 Lumbar: Spanning the transverse processes of vertebrae L-1 to L-5 Sple& Thorax N Spinal 4.117 Posterior views of vertebral column. showing cervical and lum bar intertransversarli Interspinalis Extending from the spinous processes in the cervical and lumbar regions, these short muscles help extend the spine. The cervical muscles are deep to the ligamentum nuchae while the lumbar muscles are deep to the interspinous ligament (4.118). Like the intertrausversarii, these muscles are too deep to isolate. Thoracolumbar Fascia Despite its formidalle name, the thoracolumbar fascia is Spinous process of T-9 just wliat it says it is: a broad, flat, tendinous sheatl streteh- ing across the thorax and lumbar regions. More accu- rately, the thoracclumbar fascia is a thick, diaond-shaped tendon that lies superficially across the posterior thorax, Latissimus dorsi stretches across the sacrum to the posterior iliac crest, and runs upward to the lower thoracic vertebrae (+.123). The fascia is an anchor for several muscles in the Thoracolumbar fascia thorax and hips, including the latissimus dorsi and the erector spinae group. It has a flat, dense texture that is difficult to distinguish from the deeper muscles. lliac crest Thoracolumbar fascia Sacrum (deep) 1. Parther prone. Draw out the diamond shape of tlhe fascia by locating the posterior iliac crest, the surface Splne & Thorax of the sacrum, and the lower thoracic vertebrac. 2. Using both hands, finmly grasp and lift the tissue of the low back. Note the thick layer of connective tissue underneath the skin but superficial to the erector spinae muscles. Do n1ot be surprised if the fascia is so densc that you find it difficult to grasp, let alone lift. 4.123 Posterior view of lower thorax and pelvis Ask your partner to alternatetly raise her elbows slightly and relax (this wiff contract the latissius corsi and tighten the fascia). Do you feel any change in the superficial tissue? Then move your hands laterally off the "diamond" and onto the latissimus dorsi muscle beily (4.124). Do you notice any textural differences between these two tissues? text: Head, Neck & Face 5 Mirrors, storefront windows, and smartphones offer some thing Topographical View 226 humans couldn't live without-a reflection. But let's remember that Exploring the Skin and Fascia 227 "beauty is only skin deep" and take a closer look at what lies beneath. Bones and Bony Landmarks of the Head, Neck, and Face 228 Two casily located structures are your cheekbone and jawline. Trace Overview: Bony Landmark these bones on yourself. We'll identify the bony landmarks later, but Trails 230 for now just explore their hard surfaces. What's your cheekbone's Overview: Muscles of the relationship to your eye socket? If you open and close your jaw, can Head, Neck, and Face 240 you locate where it attaches to your skull? Synergists-Muscles Working Together 242 Then palpate around the underside of your jaw and anterior neck. If you feel some unusual structures, you're right on target. (More about Muscles of the Head, Neck. and Face 244 this on p. 238.) Overview: Muscles of Facial Now clench and relax your jaw. Can you feel a pair of strong muscles Expression 261 on the sides of your cheeks? How thick do they feel? If you slide yout Other Structures of the Head, fingers up along the areas lateral to your eyes, you'll sense another pair Heck, and Face 270 of museles contracting while clenching. Examine a classroon skull and note the deep trough beneath your zygomiatic arch (cheekbone). This inuscle (temporalis) fills that entire area. Now pull out a mirror for some fun. Can you raise your eyebrows? How about just one? Can you wink your eye? Flare your lostrils? How about wiggle your ears? Why might it be easier (or harder) to activate these uscles unilaterally versus bilaterally? As you'll sooi discover, the face is covered with little muscles hard at work. 225

Since the cranial bones. with their 5.8 Inferior view tightly wovcn suturcs, arc dcsigned to protect the brain. any cursory examination of the skull would seem to support the hypothesis that these bones are immobile. In the 1920s. however, a young osteopath named William Sutherland was determined to prove that there was 2n infinitesimal, yet palpable. motion or rhythm of the cranial bones. Using himself as a guinea pig. Sutherland tested his hypothesis by applying a variety of hamemade contraptions to his head. including a football helmet with screws drilled through it. BONY LANDHARKS a 'I'rachea b Cricoid cartilage c 'T'hyroid cartilage d Hyoid bone With the progress of evolution, the skulls of more advanced creatures began to have fewer and fewer bones. For example.5ome fish have more than one hundred bones in the skull. reptiles may have seventy. and primitive mammals forty. A human skull corrtains lwenty-lwo bones, eighl of which fcrm the cranium. text: Frontal Bone Greater wings of the sphenoid Located on the anterior aspect of the cranium, the broad frontal bone forns the forehead and upper rin of the eye sockets. It articulates with the parietal bones to form the coronal sutures, which are deep to the occipitofrontalis and lateral edge of the temporalis muscles (p. 251 ). Frontal bone Partner supine. Explore the region of the forehead, moving superiorly to the coronal sutures, inferiorly to the brow, and laterally to the anterior edge of the ten poralis muscle. Sphenoid Bone The sphenoid bone is located inside the cranium and has major articulations with the fourteen bones of the skull. Located behind the eyeballs and superior to the zygomatic arches, the sphenoid is shaped like a butterfly, and its lateral portions are called the greater wings (5.15). The temporalis lies on top of thcsc flat wings, making thhem inaccessible. 5.15 Anterior view. showing sphenoid bone Head, Neck & Face location of the sphenoid bone 1. Partner supine. Place your fingers at the middle of the zygomatic arch (cheekbone) to locate thhe greater wings of thc sphenoid. 2. Slide your fingers superiorly one inch olnto the teimporalis muscle belly. The greater wing of the sphennoid is located deep to the thick temporalis belly. Facial Bones Nasal Located at the bridgc of thc nose, the small nasal bones are positioned between the frontal and maxillary bones (sec p. 228) and are virtually indistinguishable from them. Zygomatic Better knowi as the cheekbone, the zygomatic bone forms the anterior aspect of the zygoimatic arch and the lateral portion of the orbit of the eye (5.16). It serves as an attachment site for the masseter muscle. Maxilla The naxillary bones form the center of the face, the inferior portion of the orbit of the eye, the surface around the nose, and the upper jaw in which the upper row of teeth articulate. 5.16 "Dawn of the Dead"? Muscles of the Head, Neck, and Face Sternocleidomastoid The stermocleidomastoid (SCMl) is located on the lateral ald anterior aspects of the neck. It hhas a large belly with two heads: a flat, clavicular head and a slender, sternal head (5.33). Botl heads mmerge to attaelh behind the ear at the mastoid process. The carotid artery Ip. 271) passes deep and medial to the SCM; the external jugular vein lies superficial to it. The SCM is also superficial, completely accessible, and often visible when the head is turmed to the side in Lord Byron-like fashion (5.3+). The location of their attachments hints at their initial relationship: They form an almost continuous tendon along the superior nuchal line and mastoid process. Their other attachments are at either end of the clavicle. sternocleidemastoid ster-no-kfi-do-mas-toyd 244 Trail Guide to the Body

sternocleidomastoid 1. Supine with practitioner at head of the table. Locate the mastoid process of the teimporal bone, the medial claviele, and the top of the sternum. 2. Draw a line between these landmarks to delineate the location of the SCM. Note how both SCMs form a "V" on the front of the neck. 3. Ask your partner to raise his head very slightly off the table as you palpate the SCM (5.36). It will usually protrude visibly. (To make the SCM more distinct, rotate the head slightly to the opposite side and then ask your partner to flex his neck.) 4. Palpate along the borders of the SCM, following it behind the carlobe and then down to the clavicle and sternum (5.37). Seulpt around the skinny sternal tendon and the wider clavicular tendon. 5.36 Partner supine. flexing his head slightly to engage the SCM With your partner relaxed, can you grasp the SCM between your fingers and outline its thickness and shape? How much space is between the clavicular attachments of the ScM and trapezius? ft shouid be roughly two to three inches. Scalenes Anterior Middle Posterior The three scalenes are sandwiched between the sternocleidomastoid and the anterior flap of the trapezius 011 the anterior, lateral neck. Their fibers begin at the side of the cervical vertebrae, dive underneath the clavicle and attach to the first and second ribs (5.39). During normal Posterior inhalation, the scalenes perform the vital task of elevating the upper ribs. Middle The anterior scalene (5.42) lies partially tucked beneatl the sternocleidoinastoid. Tlhe middle scalene (5.43) is slightly larger and lies lateral to the anterior Anterior scalene. Both muscle bellies are directly accessible. The smaller posterior scalene (5.44) is located between the middle scalene and levator scapula. The posterior scalene First rib is positioned deeper than the other scalenes. Due to its small size and buried location, the posterior scalene can be diffcult to distinguish from tlhe surrounding bellies. Masseter Zygomatic arch The imasseter is the strongest muscle in the body relative to its size. The two masseters together exert a biting force of nearly one hundred and fifty pounds of pressure- enough to bite off a fingerl The niasseter is the primary chewing muscle and is used in speaking and swallowing. Located on the side of the mandible, the square-shaped masseter is composed of two overlapping bellies. The superficial belly can be accessed from the face (5.51); the deep belly is palpable from inside the mouth (5.52). The masseter is situated deep to the parotid gland (p. 272) yet is easily palpable. Suprahyoids and Digastric The suprahyoids (geniohyoid, mylohyoid, and stylohyoid) form a wall of muscle along the underside of the jaw (5.60, 5.61). Stretching from the edge of the mandible to the hyoid bone, they lie inferior to the glossus muscles (the muscles of the tongue). Even though each of the three suprahyoids is quite small, collectively they affect the tongue and hyoid bone and are important in chewing, swallowing, and speaking. They are partially deep to the digastric inuscle, yet are accessible. The suprahyoid bellies cannot be individually distinguished. The long. round digastric muscle is composed of a posterior and an anterior belly. The posterior belly runs froin the mastoid process to the hyoid bone (penetrating through the stylohyoid) and then loops Mylohyoid Digastric Stylahyoid Digastric through a tendinous sling on the hyoid's anterior (anterior belly) (posterior belly) surface. It continues on as the anterior belly to attach at the underside of the mandible (5.62). Both bellies are Hyoid bone Tendinous sling superficial, yet they are difficult to distinguish froim the deeper suprahyoid muscles. 5.60 Anterior/inferior view: geniohyoid is deep to mylohyoid. Platysma The platysma is a thin, superficial sheath spanning the anterior neck from the mandible to the chest (5.72). The platysma and other facial muscles are integumentary m1uscles. Instead of conmecting to bones, these muscles are embedded in the superfieial fascia and attach to the skin and overlying muscle. The platysma's clain lo fane is its ability to create the infamous "Creature from the Black Lagoon" expression. text: Galea aponeurotica Occipitofrontalis (Frontalis and Occipltalis) The occipitofrontalis is the muscle primarily responsible Frontalis for raising the forehead into an expression of surprise. It is a unique muscle with four thin bellies- two frontalis bellies on the forehead and two occipitalis bellies Occipitalis located on the back of the head. The four bellies are joined by the galea aponeurotica, a broad sheath of connective tissue stretehing across the top of the cranium (5.7+). Although the occipitofrontalis is superficial, its thin fibers cannot be isolated. Longus Capitis and Longus Colli Tucked between the trachea and the cervical vertebrae Occiput are two small muscles-longus capitis and longus colli (5.81, 5.82). Attaching from the anterior surface of the cervieal vertebrae to the occiput and atlas, they laterally C-1 flex, rotate, and Hex the head and neck. They also help to reduce the lordotic curve of the cervical vertebrae. Each muscle has a multibranched design similar to that of thie erector spinae muscles of the back. Buccinator Loeated at the center of the cheek, this strong muscle tightens thc corner of the lip and presses the cheek firnly against the teeth. Bilateral involvement will help to blow a trumpet, suck on a straw or assist in mastication by keeping food between the molars. Visually, it can produce a small dimple at the cheek's center and, with a unilateral contra.ction, generate a look of contenpt. Blowing out the cheeks 5.85 Engage the buccinators will stretch the buccinators. 5.86 Purse the lips and press the by blowing into a trumpet. cheeks against the teeth to feel the buccinator contract. Depressor Anguli Oris Head, Neck & Face The DAO pulls the corner of the mouth inferiorly and laterally. With a bilateral contraction it forms al1 inverted “clown smile." Mild engagement of the DAOs is a common. involuntary signal of sadness, while a solid. contraction is offen associated with frowning. Look for it during. both reprimands (upset parent to mischievous child) and apologies 5.87 The DAO often works in (contrite politician to skeptical public). S.88 The DAo is located just inferior and conjunction with the mentalis (p. 264). creating expressions that range lateral to the corner of the mouth, above the base of the mandible. from a pout to a scowl. Depressor Labil Inferioris Located just medial to DAO (above), the DLI depresses the corner of the mouth while protruding and drawing the lower lip laterally. By doing so, it bares the lower tceth, making it essential for brushing and flossing. The DLl can be seen at sporting events both on the field and in the slands. An athlete's DLIs will engage to display determination (for example, a soccer player sprinting for the goal) while opposing fans will use their DLls to express nock anger (especially if tlhe 5.89 The DLl, along with orbicularis 5.90 The DLl is located lateral oris (p. 265), helps to shape the player scores). to the center of the chin. mouth during speech. buecinator buk-sin-aye-tor L- trumpeter. proclaimer Isbii lay-bee-eye L. pertaining to the lips 5 Head. text: Other Structures of the Head, Neck, and Face Tliere are several accessible arteries, glands, and nerves in the head, neck, and face (5.123). Many are superficial and delicate aud should therefore be palpated gently. It is advisable to locate and explore these structures on yourself before palpatinig theim on a partner. Superficial temporal artery - Temporalis Facial nerve External auditory meatus Parotid duct Head, Neck & Face Parotid gland Facial artery Common carotid artery Submandibular gland Thyroid cartilage Cricoid cartilage Thyroid gland 5.123 Lateral view with superficial Trachea musculature removed Hurnans have unly thiree rmuscles with which tu rnove thie ears. These muscles tend to be weak. and on some of us. they are not even functional. Horses, on the other hand, have thirteen muscles that perfor m a variety of ear movements. Why? Humans communicate their feelings through facial expressions ard not by wiggling their ears. Ilarses. however. display their emotions prirmarily with their ears. so they need a strong. diverse group of muscles to create specific actions and expressions. 270Trail Guide to the Body

Common Carotid Artery The carotid artery is the primary supplier of blood to the head and neck. It ascends the anterior and lateral sides of the neck and lies deep to the stermocleidoimastoid (SCM) and infrahvoid muscles. Its strong pulse caii be felt medial to the SCM at the level of the hyoid bone. SCM Common carotid artery Carotid 1. Partncr supine or seated. Place two fingerpads at thc artery (deep) angle of the mandible. 2. Slide off the angle in an inferior and medial direction and press gently into the neck (5.12+). The strong pulse of the carotid artery should be quite noticeable. Are you medial to the SCM? Are you under the mandible at the fevel of the hyoid bone? 5.124 Partner supine, feeling the pulse of the carotid artery Superficial Temporal Artery Temporalis The superficial temporal artery branches off thc cxternal carotid artery and crosses over the top of the zygomatic arch. It continues superiorly along the side of the cranium, Jying superficial to the temporalis muscle. Tlhe pulse of the Head, Neck & Face superficial temporal artery can be detected in front of the ear along lhe zygomatic arch. [ Superficlal temporal artery Superficial 1. Partner supine or seated. Place your fingerpad in front temporal of the ear at the zygomatic arch (5.125). artery 2. Gently explore and palpate for the artery's pulse. If you do not feel it, adjust your finger position and make sure your pressure is not too deep. 5.12S Partner supine Facial Artery The facial artery is a small, superficial vessel that branches off from the external carotid artery and curves around the base of the mandible (jawline) toward the mouth and nose. Its pulse may be difficult to detect but can be felt along the base of the mandible at the anterior edge of the masseter. Faclal artery Base of the mandible 1. Partner supine. With your partner clenching her jaw, locate the masseter's anterior edge. 2. Position your finger next to the base of the mandible and gently palpate for the pulse of the artery (5.126). Facial artery Are you at the base of the mandible, along the anterior edge of the mrasseter? Masseter 5.126 carotid ka-rot-id Grk causing deep sleer 5Head.Neck & Face 271

text: External auditory meatus Facial Nerve The facial nerve is not a structure you will want to palpate specifically, but, because of its proximity to other palpable structures on the side of the face, you will need to be aware of its location. The facial nerve (cranial nerve Vll) exits from the cranium and emerges superficially just anterior to the mastoid process. As it passes beneath the parotid gland, the nerve branches off and spreads across the face, scalp, alld neck (5.127). Often two or more branclhes of tl1e facial nerve cross superficially over the zygomatic arch. When exploring the parotid gland, masseter, or zygomatic arch, lbe mindful of the facial nerve's presence. Static pressure on the nerve can cause irritation or inflammation, or even induce nausea in your partner. Parotid gland / Vertebral Artery 5.i27 Lateral view, showing branches of the facial nerve The vertebral artery branches off the subclavian artery and supplies blood to the brain and spinal cord. It ascends thie meck tliruugli the transrerse furauel of C-G to C-1 before passing through the foramen magnum of the occiput (5.128). It is inaccessible, of course, but a vital struclure to be awarc of when palpating and/or passively Head, Neck & Face moving the head and neck. Yertebral Parotid Gland and Duct, artery Submandibular Gland There are three salivary glands in the neck and face: TVPs of the parotid, submandibular, and sublingual. All are cervical accessible, but be aware that palpation of thie salivary vertebrae glands can stimulate the production of saliva. The parotid gland is located in front of the earlobe, superficial to the masseter muscle (p. 250). It has a soft, lumpy surface and is penetrated by branches of th1e facial nerve. The parotid duct is a spaghetti-sized tube cxtending anteriorly from the parotid gland. It tucks around the anterior edge of the masseter to funnel saliva to the mouth. True to its name, the submandibular gland is tucked underneath the base of the mandible. Its round shapc can 5.128 Anterior view of neck and head he located anterior to the angle of the mandible. Cervical Lymph Nodes The numerous hundles of lymph nodes in the cervical region are divided into two groups: superficial and deep. The superficial cervical nodes (5.132) are located primarily along the underside of the mandible, posterior and inferior to the earlobe. and in the posterior triangle (p. 226) between the platysima ald the deep fascia. Tlte deep cervical nodes are larger and lie beside several large vessels and glands. Both the superficial and deep lymph nodes are slightly movable and have the size and texture of soft lentils or moist raisins. They are often Occipital and mastoid nodes tender on palpation. Cervical lymph nodes Submandibular 1. Supine or seated. Place your fingers on the lateral side nodes of the neck. Using your broad fingerpads, gently palpate 5.132 under the skin for the superficial cervical nodes. SCM 2. Explore along the underside of the mandible and il1 the posterior triangle. Once you have located a node, Supraclavicular - carefully outline its size and shape. nodes Are they slightly movable and the size and texture of soft fentils? Exploring the Skin and Fascia Thigh Partner supine. Begin by placing your hands on your partner's thigh. Explore from the pelvis to the knee, sensing the temperature of the tissue. Be sure to access the thigh's medial and lateral sides as well. 2. Sink your hands into the thigh and gently twist the tissue in opposite directions (6.+). Take particular note of its thickness and elasticity. For example, the skin and fascia just proximal to the knee may be thinner than the tissue near the pelvis. 6.4 Partrer supine, medial view of right thigh Posterior knee 1. Partner prone. Holding the ankle, passively flex the knee. Use your other hand to gently grasp the skin and fascia proximal to the posterior knee. As you roll it betwecn your fingers, note the tissue's pliability and texture. 2. Compare what you have felt to the thicker tissue of thc medial thigh and the denser fascia of the lateral thigh. 3. Still grasping the skin and fascia, passively flex and extend the knee (6.5). Feel the tissue stretch as the knee extends. Continue this same movement while grasping the sides of the thigh. If the tissue is diffcult to grasp, you may want to use the flat of your hand to Pelvls & Thigh sennse the changes in the tissue. 6.5 Partner prone, posterior/medial view of right thigh Buttocks 1. Partner prone. Using the back of your hand, explorc the temperature of the posterior and lateral buttocks. Since thie buttocks are couposed of botli large inuscles and large quanhities of adipose, they are a good area for exploring tissue differences. Trail1 Solo Pass Anterior Superior Iliac Spine (ASIS) As the name suggests, the ASIs is located on the anterior and superior aspect of the ilium. Both ASISs are the superficial tips located below the waistline. The ASIS serves as the attachment site for the sartorius imuscle alnd the inguinal ligament. (Note: Prior to the 1mid-1990s, when pants were designed and worn in a manner that fit the pelvic region, the ASISs were located "underneath the front pants pockets." Nowadays, with the waistbands of both young men and women rarely seen “north" of the mid-gluteal cleft, this helpful hint no longer applies.) ASIS 1. Trail 3 Tailbone Trail Sacrum Median Sacral Crest Edge of the Sacrun The sacrum is a large, triangular bone at the inferior end of the vertebral column. Situated between the Median sacral crest overhanging sides of the pelvis, the sacrum is made up of a series of four or five vertebrae that are fused together. Running down the center of the sacrum and composed of three to four points is the median sacral crest. On either side of the median sacral crest is the lateral sacral crest --a smaller series of bony knobs. The edge of the sacrum is part of the attachment site for the gluteus maximus and the sacrotuberous ligament. Although the sacrum's bumpy surface lies deep to the thoracolumbar fascia and sacroiliac ligaments, it is easily accessible. sacrum 1. Partner prone. Place a thumb and finger upoli eacli PSIS and explore between and below these points for the surface of the sacrum. 2. Locate the midline of the sacrum and explore the points of the sacral crest (6.31). Palpate superiorly to the level of the PSIS and just above the coccyx. 6.31 Posterior view, using the PSISs as guides to 3. Slide your fingers laterally off the side of the sacrun, locate the median sacral crest of the sacrum pressing your fingertips into its solid edge (6.32). Follow the lateral edge up toward the PSIS and dowl to the coccyx. How many small tips can you feel along the sacraf crest? Can you folow both tateral edges inferiorly to where they PSISs Sacrum converge at the coccyx? ff you move laterally from the cuter Pelvis & Thigh edge of the sacrum. can you feel the mass of the giuteus maximus (p. 315)? Reptiles and most birds have two sacral vertebrae, whereas mammals have between three and five Humans in particular have more because, as upright creatures, the entire weight of the upper body is transferred through the sacrum to the pelvis and legs. All that remains of the spinous processes of the sacrum's vertebrae are the time-warn tips of the median sacral crest. 6.32 Posterior/lateral view. partner prone, exploring the edge of the sacrum sacrum WY- L. sacred or holy thing. from the use of the sacrum in Roman animal sacrifice 290 Trail Guide to the Body

Trail5 The Underpass Umbilicus The umbilicus (or navel) will, of course, be visible when the abdomen is undraped. When not exposed, the umbilicus can be felt at the midline of the body, superior to the level of the ASISs. Umbilicus Pubic Crest and Tubercles (Refer fo p. 286 for more information.) The pubic tubercles are located on the superior aspect of the pubic crcst. Each tubercle is shaped likc a small hom and serves as an attachiment site for the adductor longus muscle and the inguinal ligament. The tubercles might be one to two inches apart and are not always easy to palpate. Pubic crest and tubercles 6.38 Anterior/lateral view. partner supine 1. Face your partmer as he lies supine on the table. Set your fingertips on his umbilicus, allowing your palm to rest on the abdomcn. Thc hecl of your hand will be on (or just superior to) the pubic crest. 2. Tum your hand and relocate the crest with your fingers (6.38). Explore its lorizontal ridge. Remember that the pubic crest is the only horizontal stretch of bone in this vicinity. 3. Move laterally and explore for the tips of the pubic tubercles. Palpate both tubercles, noting the distance between theml. Do you feel a firm. bony prominence inferior and medial to the level of the ASiS? With respect to the pubic tubercles. are the bony prominences you feel on the superior Pelvls & Thigh part of the pubic crest? Are the tubercies on the same level as the greater trochanters? Superior Ramus of the Pubis The superior ramus of the pubis spans 45′ from the pubic tubercle toward the AlIS. It forms a ridge that serves as an attachment site for the pectineus muscle (p. 319). Because it is deep to the inguinal ligament and a neurovascular bundle, the superior ramus can be challenging to palpate. Superlor ramus 1. Partner supine. Place your flexed knee under your partner's knee. This position will flex and laterally rotate the hip, allowing for easier palpation. 2. Locate the pubic crest. Slide laterally off the crest toward the AllS. Sink into the tissue, feeling for the buried ridge of the superior ramus (6.39). Are you lateral and sfightly superior to the pubic tubercie? if you cannot feel the edge of the ramus. The ramus of the pubis, the anterior half of the bridge, serves as an attachment site for 6.40 Adductor muscle attachment sites along the gracilis and adductor brevis muscles; both rami are the rami attachment sites for the adductor magnus muscle (6.40). Hamstrings Biceps Femoris Semitendinosus Semimembranosus The hamstrings are located along the posterior thigh between the vastus lateralis and adductor magnus (6.68). Comparatively, the hamstrings are not as massive as the quadriceps femoris group. but are nonetheless strong hip extensors and knee flexors. All three hamstrings have a common origin at the ischial tuberosity. Their tubular bellies extend superficially down the thigh before bccoming long, thin tendons that stretch across the postericr knee. As a group, the hamstrings and their distal tendons are easily palpable. Sem itendinosus Long head of Biceps femoris is the lateral hamstring. It has two biceps femoris heads- a superficial long head and a deeper, indiscemible Semimembranosus short head (6.69, 6.70). The medial hamstrings include the Short head of tvo “semi" muscles. The semitendinosus lies superficial to biceps femoris the wider and deeper semimembranosus (6.71, 6.72). 6.68 Posterior view of right thigh, showing superficial hamstrings Long head af biceps femoris Pelvls & Thigh Outline of short head Outline of long head 6.69 Posterior view Short head of biceps femorls The term "hamstring" or ginatec ir 1stin-century 6.70 Posterior view England. Adductors as a group 1. Partner supine with the hip slightly flexed and laterally rotated. Place your hand along the medial thigh ino. jsear duul s!u lnppe ci iaed ano. yse pun resistance (6.97). Do you feel the adductors tighten? 2. Ask your partner to altermately adduct and relax as you palpate proximally to the adductor tendons. Then move distally, exploring anterior and postcrior to the edges of the adductor bellies. Are you on the medial side of the thigh? Explore either side of the adductors to determine if you are betweer the hamstrings and quadriceps femoris group (6.98). You should be. 6.97 Partner supine. palpating the adductors as a group. line indicating cross section Gracilis and adductor longus 1. Partner supine with the hip slightly flexed and laterally rotated. Place the flat of your hand at the middle of the medial thigh. Ask your partner to adduet his hip slightly. 2. While your partner contracts, slide your fingers proximally to the pubic bone and locate the taut, prominent tendon(s) of the gracilis and adductor longus extending off of (or near) the pubic tubercle. 3. Strum your fingertip across this tendon and follow it distally as it develops into muscle tissue (6.99}. If the muscle belly slowly angles into the medial thigh, youl are palpating adductor longus. If the belly is slender and continues down the medial thigh toward the knee, you are accessing gracilis. The TFL attaches to the iliotibial tract along with the gluteus maximus. lliotibial tract The iliotibial tract (sometimes known as the iliotibial band) is a superhcial sheet of fascia with vertical fibers that run along the lateral thigh. lt emerges from the N Superior gluteal L4, 5. S1 gluteal fascia, is wide and dense over the vastus lateralis muscle (p. 306), and funnels into a strong cable along the side of the knee before inserting at the tibial tubercle (see box, p. 351). The iliotibial tract lias a thick, ualted texture (similar to packing tape) that makes it a strong stabilizing component of the hip and knee. The iliotibial tract is entirely accessible. The distal cable portion, anterior to the biceps femoris tendon, is the easiest part of the iliotibial tract to isolate. Tendons of the Posterior Knee Five distinct tendons are located on the posterior aspect of the knee (6.110). Biceps femoris and the iliotibial tract are loeated on the lateral/posterior knee; sartorius, gracilis, ald seimitendinosus are bundled together on the medial/ posterior knee. These three tendons merge distally at rtencingsus the proximal, medial shaft of the tibia to become the pes aserinus tendon. Where is the semiimembranosus tendon? Its distal tendon is very short and deep to the semitendinosus and gracilis. Side lying. Ask your partner to flex and hold his knee Biceps femoris and iliotibial tract at 45'. The tendons will become taut in this positioil. For greater clarity, place your hand on the ankle and Head of the fibula give your partner some resistance. 2. The most prominent tendons will be biceps femoris and semitendinosus. Unlike the biceps femoris, the iliotibial tract is broad and located on the lateral side of the thigh. Medial tendons 1. Supine. Move to the medial side of the knee and palpate 6.111 Partner slde lying. lateral vlew the thin, prominent tendon of the semitendinosus. of right knee, isolating the biceps femoris and illotibial tract 2. Compared to the long, skinny tendons of semi-tendinosus and gracilis, the sartorius tendon is vider (6.112). For tlhis reason, it may be challenging to isolate. 4. Follow the three tendons distally as they blend together to become the pes anserinus tendon, which attaches on the proximal, medial shaft of the tibia. Inguinal Lymph Nodes Distal to the inguinal ligament, the superficial inguinal lymph nodes are easily palpable. They number between eight and ten and vary in size from a small lentil to a raisin. Inguinal lymph nodes 1. Partner supine, with the knee bolstered. 'T'his position will flex and laterally rotate the hip, allowing for easier palpation. 2. Locate the inguinal ligament. Slide inferiorly and explore for the superficial nodes (6.1+7). Sacrotuberous Ligament 6.147 Partner supine. palpating the inguinal lymph nodes This broad, solid ligament stretches between the ischial tuberosity and the edge of the sacrunn. Although it is deep to the gluteus maximus muscle, it is distinctly palpable and might feel like a span of bone. Gluteus maximus (cut Sacrotuberous ligament and reflected) 1. Partner prone. Locate the ischial tuberosity. Locate the edge of the sacrum (p. 290). 2. Slide your fingertips off the tuberosity toward the edge of the sacrun. Using firni pressure, palpate througlt the gluteus maximus belly and strum broadly across the ligament (6.148). text: Bones of the Knee, Leg, and Foot T'he knee is formed by the articulation of the distal femur and proximal tibia (7.S). The tibiofemoral (knee) joint, the largest synovial joint in the body, is a mcdified hinge joint. It is capable of flexion and extension; and when the knee is in a flexed position, it can medially Femur and laterally rotate the knee (p. 348). The region of the knee also includes the snall patella Patella ("kneecap") and the proximal fibula. The bony surfaces Tibiofemoral !o! of the knee are superficial and easily accessible. The tibia and fibula are the bones of the leg. The Proximal tibia ("shinbone") runs superficially from the knee to tibiofibular joint the ankle just as the ulna runs superficially from the elbow to the wrist. 'T'he fibula's relationship to the libia is also similar to the radius's relationship to the ulna: it is lateral to the tibia and virtually deep to thc surrounding Fibula muscles. The fbula bears only 10% of the body's weight Tibia and rightfully so: it is the thinnest bone in the body in proportion to its length. Calcaneus Talurs. Trail1 The Back Road Lateral and Medial Malleoli The lateral and medial malleoli are the large, conspicuous knobs on either side of the ankle. Thhe broader medial malleolus is located at the distal end of the tibia, while the more slender lateral malleolus protrudes from the distal fibula. Lateral and medlal malleoll Partner seated or supine. Explore and compare the shapes and sizes of the two malleoli. Palpating all sides of their surfaces, note how the lateral malleolus extends further distally than its medial counterpart (7.31). Sliding proximally. can you connect the medial malleotus to the shaft of the tibia and then to the tibiar tuberosity? Is the medial malleolus more proximal than the lateral? 7.31 Palpating the level of each malleolus Malleolar Grooves Both the medial and lateral malleoli have small vertical grooves carved into their posterior surfaces. These grooves are designed to offer stability and leverage to tendons that Medial malleolar groove bend around the ankle. Because these tendons lie either Medial malleolus inside the groove or beside it, it can bc diffieult to feel the actual depression of the groove. Malleolar grooves 1. Supine or seated. Locate the medial malleolus. 2. Slide roughly half an inch posteriorly to palpate the posterior aspect of the malleolus for a slender, vertical groove (7.32). 3. Shorten the surrounding tissue by passively inverting the foot and explore the length of the medial groove and the superficial tendons. 7.32 Posterior/medial 4. Try this same method along the lateral malleolus, view of right foot only now, passively evert the foot to shorten the sur- rounding tissue and locate the lateral groove (7.33). Because each groove runs vertically. can you rofl your finger horizontally across each vertical groove to Leg&Foot determine its tocation and shape? Since we are no longer tree climbers, our foot has lost its handlike capabilitics in order to become a platform for an upright body. This does not mean, however, that the toes were designed to be inactive. An infant's foot has twenty times the toe-grasping capacity of a shoe-wearing adull, And ir shueless cultures, people relain the prehensile abilities of their feet throughout ad ulthood, using them for sewing and even threading needles. 7Leg&Foot359

text: Trail3 The Archway Medial, Middle, and Lateral Cuneiforms Thie three cuneiforms lie in a row bebween the navicular and metalarsals. 'The medial cuneiform serves as all attachnent for the tibialis anterior and tibialis posterior muscles. It can be isolatcd along its dorsal and mcdial Talus surfaces. T'he middle and lateral cuneiforms, sandwiched Navicular bctween the niedial cuneiform and tlie cuboid, arc Cuneiforms accessible on their dorsal surfaces. Cuneiforms 1. Partner seated or supine. Locate the base of tlhe first Imetatarsal. 2. Glide proximally to the skinny ditch of the first tarsometatarsal joint. Continue proximally onto thic surface of the nedial cuneiform. 3. Slide laterally from the medial cuneiform along the dorsal surface of tlhie foot and cxplore the surfaces of the imiddle and lateral cuneiforms (7.50). Are you proximal to the base of the first metatarsal and can you isolate the joint between these two bones? f you follow the tibialis anterior tendon. does it lead to the same location at which you were palpating the medial cuneiform? The tibialis anterior tendon (p. 37S) runs supcr- ficially down the dorsal surface of the ankle and leads direetly to the medial side of the medial cunciforn. I Have 7.50 Dorsal/lateral view of right foot your partner dorsiflex his foot and follow the tendon distally as it blcnds into the mcdial cunciform. Mammals such as cats and dogs are called digiti- grades. mcaning they walk on thcir tocs (digits). When digitigrades are standing. their tarsals and metatarsals are off the ground, forming what appears to be the leg. For this reason, the ankle of a dog or Leg&Foot cat is often mistaken for the knee. while the actual Knee knee appears to be hyperextended. Digitigrades are raised up on their toes this way for additional height. The extra height enhances sensory Hind leg perception and increases stride length. Knee of a cow Hooved animals. call unguligrades, go a step further than digitigrades in lifting themselves up on all their phalanges except the distal one. With this wide. four-point stance, these animals literally walk Hnd teg Ankle on the tips of their toes all the time. Ropejo Walking "tippy-toe" will quickly tell you that neither of these designs work for humans. We are Ankle plantigrades. meaning we walk on the soles of our Metatarsals feet. As we are also bipeds. we must spread our Metatarsals feet out. pressing all of our foot bones firmly on the ground in order to keep our balance. cuneiform ku-ne-i-fosm L. cuneus. wedge-shaped 364 Trail Guide to the Body

Extensors of the Ankle and Toes Tibialis Anterior Extensor Digitorun Longus Tibial tuberosity Extensor Hallucis Longus These extrinsic muscles are located on the anterior aspect of the leg between the shaff of the tibia and the fibularis Tibialis muscles. The tendons of all three muscles cross benneath anterior the extensor retinacula at the ankle (p. 403). The tibialis anterior is large, superficial, and the most clearly isolated of the group. It lies directly lateral to the tibial shaft (7.81). Squeezed between the tibialis anterior and the fibularis muscles, the extensor digitorum longus is partially superficial. Its four tendons are clearly palpable on the dorsal surface of the foot (7.82, 7.83). The muscle belly of the extensor hallucis longus lies 7.81 Anterior/medial view of right leg and foot deep to the other two muscles and can be accesscd only indirectly (7.84); however, like the extensor digitorum, its distal tendon is easily found on. the dorsal surface of the foot as it leads toward the first toe. Because they bear the weight of the body when standing. walking. and running. the feet are sometimes known as the "little soldiers." In comparison to standing. walking increases the pressure on the feet twofold while running increases it fourfold. These stresses demand that the foot be designed for more than lying flat and idle on the ground. Thus Adductor Flexor the bones and ligaments of the foot are arranged to hallucis hallucis form three arches-the medial longitudinal, late:al brevis longitudinal, and transverse. These arches connect with three points of contact-the calcaneus and the heads of the first and fifth metatarsals. Flexor digiti The three arches together raise the center of the minimi brevis foot. creating a structure that is ideally shaped to distribute and absarb the weight of the body. The arches also help the plantar surface of the foot adapt Cuboid to uneven terrain while hiking or climbing. G Fibular and Tiblal Collateral Llgaments The fibular collateral ligament is a strong. thin strap that crosses the knee joint running from the lateral epicondyle of the femur to the head of the fibula (7.118). lt is supcrficial and located betwccn the biccps femoris tendon Lateral epicondyle and the iliotibial tract. The broad tibial collateral ligament lics supcrficial to the joint capsule of the knee, but imay not be as easy to Fibular collateral isolate as its lateral counterpart (7.118). Stretching nearly ligament two inches distal to the knee joint, it is deep to the pes anserinus tendon. Head of Both collateral ligaments resist medial rotation of the the fibula knee. The fibular collateral also stabilizes the knee against genu varum stresses (often seen in bowlegged cowboys) while tlhe tibial collateral protects against genu valguni (knock-knee) stresses. An exainple would be a blow from a football helmet to tlie lateral side of the kee joit. [ Flbular collateral ligament 7.122 Lateral view of right knee 1. Partner seated with knee flexed. Locate the head of the fibula and the lateral epicondyle (7.122). 2. Slide your finger between these points and gently strum in a horizontal direction across this superficial ligament. Is the band of tissue you feel the width of a pencil? Does it run from the epicondyle to the fibular head? Is it anterior to the biceps fermoris tendon? Ask your partner to cross his leg so the ankle is resting on top of the opposite knee. This position will allow the fibular collateral ligaiment to be easily accessed. Roll your finger between the epicondyle and the head of the fibula and palpate the ligament (7.123). Tibial collateral ligament Menisci of the Knee Thc mcnisci are filbrocartilaginous discs attached to the tibial condyles (7.120). They not only are im portant for weight distribution and friction reduction, but also help the round femoral condyles sit comfortably upon the flat tibial plateaus. The edge of the medial meniscus can be 7.124 Medial view of right knee palpated just above the edge of the medial tibial plateau. Tlhe smaller, more mobile lateral meniscus is difficult to access. [] Medial meniscus 1. Partner seated with the knee flexed. Place your thumb superior to the medial tibial plateau in the joint space between the femur and tibia. 2. Grasp the leg with your other lhand and slowly rotate the knee medially (7.125). 3. As the medial side of the tibia rotates posteriorly, the edge of the medial meniscus will be pushed anteriorly into your thumb. The sensation may be quite subtle -a gentle pressure against your thumbpad. Bursae of the Knee Bursa under head Tremendous pressures, repetitive movements, and chafing of gastrocnemius tendons constantly challenge the knee joint. Nearly a Quadriceps dozen bursae help to protect the joint and its surrounding femoris tendon structures. The primary bursae are included here (7.126). Lying superficial to the patella, the prepatellar bursa Femur helps the skin to move freely over the patella, even with the knee flexed. Excessive kneeling and squatting Patella can inflame the vulnerable bursa, a condition called "liousemaid's knee”" (or "carpenter's knee," depending oll Prepatellar bursa your vocation). The subcutaneous infrapatellar bursa and the deep infrapatellar bursa are located on either side of the Patellar ligament patellar ligament. The deep bursa reduces friction between the tendon and the surface of the tibia. The superficial bursa can become irritated with prolonged Subcutaneous kneeling, sometimes called "parson's knee" (or infrapatellar bursa "Buddhist's knee," depending on your faith). The pes anserine bursa serves as a buffer between the Deep infrapatellar bursa pes anserinus tendons (those of the gracilis, sartorius, and semitendinosus) and the tibia. Tibia When inflamed, the superficial bursae of the knee are palpable and sometimes visible; under normal conditions, 7.126 Lateral cross section of the knee. however, they are impalpable. pes anserine bursa not shown Popliteal Artery The popliteal artery branches from the femoral artery to pass through the popliteal fossa at the back of the knee. It is situated deep in the fossa and, for this reason, its pulse can be diffieult to detect. Popliteal artery 1. Partner supine. Flex your partmer's knee in order to soften the overlying tissues. Hold the knee so that the fingertips of both hands are at the midline of the posterior knee. 2. Sink your fingerpads deep into the popliteal fossa and explore for the subtle pulse (7.127). If the puise is undetectable. follow the same instructions Leg&Foot with your partner prone. 7.127 Lateral view of right knee. feeling the pulse of the popliteal artery 396 Trail Guide to the Body

text: Deltoid Ligament Medial malleolus The deltoid ligament is composed of several ligaments Deltoid ligament that originate at the medial malleolus and fan distally Navicular tubercle to attach at the talus, sustentaculum tali, and navicular (7.1+0). It is designed to protect agaiust nedial stress of the talocrural joint. The deltoid ligament is deep to the flexor retinaculum and flexor tendons (p. 38l), yet is palpable. Deltoid lgament 1. Partner supine or seated. Locate the medial malleolus and sustentaculum tali. 2. Place your finger between these points and strum horizontally to isolate the fibers of the ligaments. 3. Slide distally from the medial malleolus at a +5° angle and palpate its angled fibers to define the anterior and posterior aspects of the deltoid ligament (7.1+l ). Plantar calcaneonavicular (spring) ligament Sustentaculum tali Are you palpating in the space between the medial maffeorus and sustentaculum tali? Do the fibers you feel fan 7.140 Medial view of right ankle out from the medial mafleolus and have a firm. dense texture? Plantar Calcaneonavicular (Spring) Ligament The spring ligament is a small, tough band of tissue that plays an important role in stabilizing thc medial longitudinal areh of the fcot (7.1+0). I.ocated along the medial side of the foot, the ligament stretehes from the sustentaculum tali to the navicular tubercle and might be positioned deep to the tibialis posterior tendon. The spring ligament might be extremely tender and should be accessed slowly. Be sure to comnunicate with your parmer. Spring ligament 1. Supine or seated. Passively invert the foot to soften any surrounding tissue and locate the sustentaculum tali 7.141 Deltoid ligament and navicular tbercle. 2. Palpating between these bony landmarks, use a fingertip to slowly explore the taut surface of the spring ligament (7.142). Leg&Foot Are you between the sustentaculum tali and navicular tubercle? Can you rofl your fingertip slowly across the surface of the ligament? 7.142 Medial view. palpating the spring ligament 7Leg& Foot 401
section: 7.142 Medial view. palpating the spring ligament

text: Lateral Collateral Ligaments of the Ankle To understand why the ankle is the most frequently inju red articulation in the body, you m ight begin with tlhe ankle's lateral collateral ligaments. Designed to stabilize 7.143 Lateral view thc distal end of the fibula to thc calcaneus and talus, of right ankle these three bands are the most often tormn ligaments during ankle sprains. They are nowhere near as strong as the deltoid ligament on the ankle's medial side and are most vulnerable when the foot is inverted and plantar flexed. The anterior talofibular ligament (7.1+3, a) spans from the anterior surface of the lateral malleolus to the lateral side of the talus's neck. When the foot is weight- Lateral malleolus bearing and excessively inverted, this ligament is often the first to be torn. The posterior talofibular ligament (b) is much 7.144 Lateral view of right ankle. palpating the anterior in1 the most severe of ankle injuries, such as disloealious. talofibular ligament It attaches to the posterior side of the lateral malleolus and the talus's lateral tubercle. Between these two ligaments is the calcaneofibular ligament (c). It spans from the lateral malleolus down to the lateral surface of the calcaneus, deep to the fibularis tendons. Only during severe ankle sprains, after the anterior talofibular has ripped, will this ligament be torn as well. ] Lateral collateral ligaments of the ankle 1. Partner supine or seated. 2. Anterior talofibular (7.14t): This ligament is not very distinguishable, but its position can be isolated by first locating the anterior aspect of the lateral malleolus. Slide your thumb toward the head of the talus (p. 360), roughly an inch. The ligament passes just medial to the extensor digitorum brevis belly (p. 387). 3. Posterior talofibular (7.145): Set your finger on the posterior side of the lateral malleolus. Continue 7.145 Posterior view of around the mallelous to the surface of the talus's right ankle, palpating lateral tubercle. (If you reach the cal caneal tendon, the posterior talofibular you have gone too far.) Between these landmarks will ligament be the ligament. Also, use gentle pressure here since Leg&Foot this region can often be tender. 4. Calcaneofibular (7.l+6): Locate the distal end of the lateral malleolus and the lateral aspect of the calcaneus. This ligament runs at a slight oblique angle and passes posterior to the fibular tubercle. 7.146 Lateral view of right ankle. palpating the calcaneofibular ligament 402 Trail Guide to the Body

Retinacula of the Ankle The tendons of the extensor muscles (p. 378) are Superior fibular Superior extensor supported by the superior and inferior extensor retinacula. retinaculum retinaculum The superior extensor retinaculum is broad and crosses the front of the ankle just proximal to the malleoli. The Lateral Medial malleolus inferior extensor retinaculum is "Y" shaped and begins malleolus distal to the lateral malleolus on the calcaneus. It spans Inferior extensor the ankle and then divides, with one fork attaching at Inferior fibular retinaculum retinaculum the medial malleolus and the other connecting to the navicular (7.147). The fibularis muscles are stabilized by the superior and inferior fibular retinacula. The superior fibular retinaculum stretches from the lateral malleolus to tl1e 7.147 Dorsal view of calcaneus, and the inferior fibular retinaculum pins the right foot and ankle fibularis tendons down at the fibular tubercle. The flexor retinaculum is a broad strap extending from the medial calcaneus to the medial malleolus. It is designed to hold in place the tendons of the flexor muscles and the tibial artery and nerve (7.150). [Extensor retinacula 1. Partner supine. Ask your partner to dorsiflex her ankle and extend her toes. The pressure from the bulging tendons will make the retinacula more pronounced. 2. Palpate an inch proximal to the medial malleolus for the broad fibers of the superior extensor retinaculum. 3. Locate the inferior extensor retinaculum by moving distally to the level of the malleolus (7.l+8). Explore either side of the large tibialis anterior tendon for 7.148 Partner easy access to this retinaculum. extending her toes Are the fibers you are accessing superficial and perpendicular to the extensor tendons? Ask your partner to relax her ankle and notice how the retinacula soften. Fibular retinacula 1. Ask your partner to evert her foot. The tension from the fibularis tendons will make the retinacula more pronounced. Lateral 2. Locate the fibularis tendons between the lateral malleolus Leg&Foot malleolus and lateral calcaneus (7.1+9). Roll your fingers along either side of the fibularis tendons to feel the small, short fibers of the retinaeula. Fibularis tendons (cut) For the superior retinaculum. does the fissue you fee! strap across the fibularis tendons from the lateral caicaneus to the lateral mafleolus? For the inferior retinacurum. do you feel a short band crossing over the fibular Fibular retinacula tubercle? 7.149 Lateral view of right ankle. partner everting foot retinaculum ret-i-nak-u-lun L. halter, band.rope retinacula ret-i-nsk-u-la plural for retinaculum 7Leg&Foot403

text: Flexor tendons Flexor retinaculum Medial malleolus 1. Ask your partner to dorsiflex and invert lier foot. The tension from the flexor tendons will elevate the Flexor retinaculum retinaculum closer to the surface. 2. Locate the medial malleolus and the medial side of the calcaneus. 3. Palpate between these landmarks, strumming across the broad, superficial fibers of the flexor retinaculum (7.150). Are you between the medial calcaneus and medial malfeolus? Continue to explore fhe retinaculum with the foot relaxed. Plantar Aponeurosis 7.150 Medial view of right ankle The plantar aponeurosis is a thick, superficial band of fascia stretching from the heel to the ball of the foot 17.151). Originating from the tuberosity of the calcaneus and expanding toward the metatarsal heads, it is impor- tant for supporting the longitudinal arch of the foot. Because the aponeurosis is located between the skin and muscles of the foot, it can be difficult to isolate from the surrounding tissues. 7.151 Piantar view of right foot Plantar aponeurosis 1. Partner seated or supine. Crossing the ball of the foot, draw an imaginary triangle extending down to the heel. 2. Within this triangle explore the superficial layers of Plantar aponeurosis tissue along the sole of the foot. Passively flex and extend the toes, noting how this movement affects the tension of the plantar aponeurosis. Calcaneus Posterior Tibial Artery The posterior tibial artery extends from the popliteal artery. It is superficial and its pulse can be felt just inferior and posterior to the medial malleolus. Leg&Foot Posterior tbial artery Flexor Partner supine. Locate the medial malleolus. Llsing two tendons (cut) fingerpads, slide posterior to the nalleolus and feel for tlhc pulse of the artery (7.152). Posterior tibial artery 7.152 Medial view of right ankle dorsalis pedis  dor-sal-is peh-dis L. dorsum. back. pedis, foot aponeurosis SIs-oJ-nu-D-de Grk. apo.from + neuron. nerve or tendon 404Trail Guide to the Body

Dorsalls Pedis Artery Located between: the first and second metatarsal lbones, the dorsalis pedis artery lies superficial along the dorsal Dorsalis pedis artery side of the foot. ] Dorsalis pedis artery Partner supine. Locate the first and second metatarsals. Place two fingerpads between the two bones and, using gcntle prcssure, cxplore for thc pulse of the dorsalis pedis artery (7.153). Are you lateral to the extensor hallucis longus tendon? If the puise is undetectable, move slightly lateral. 7.153 Dorsal view of right foot Sesamoid Bones of First Metatarsal The sesamoid bones of the first metatarsal are localed along the plantar surface of the first metatarsal head. Usually there are bwo of these bones, but sometines more are present. The sesamoids are spherical and imbedded in the tendon of the flexor hallucis brevis. Often only their location and density, not their specific shapes, are palpable. Sesamoid bones of first metatarsal Sesarmoid bones 1. Partner seated or supine. Locate the head of the first metatarsal. Slide around to its plantar su rface at the ball of the foot. 2. Using your thumbpad, explore this surface for the 7.154 Plantar view small sesamoid bones. Passively flex and extend the first toe to soften the surrounding tissues (7.154). Calcaneal Bursae The calcaneal bursa (7.155, a) is located between the attachment of the calcaneal tendon and the overlying skin. The retrocalcaneal bursa (b) is positioned on the Calcaneal tendon opposite side of the calcaneal tendon. It serves to cushion the tendon against the calcaneus. Both bursae are impalpable; however, they can become thick or inflamed. from tight shoes and, especially, from Leg&Foot high heels. Calcaneal bursae 1. Partner seated or supine. With the ankle in a neulral position, locate the calcaneal tendon. 2. Follow it distally until it merges into the calcaneus. Gently squeeze the skin posterior to the tendon. This is where the caleaneal bursa is located (7.155). 3. Shift your fingers between the tendon and calcaneus. If the bursae are healthy, they will not be palpable. 7.155 Medial view of right foot 7Leg&Foot405

262-267: The inferior glenohumeral region (103, 131) is related to the Levator palpebrae superioris muscle (268). The inguinal region (168, 296, 336, 337, 339) is associated with metatarsophalangeal points (346, 355, 370, 400), as well as the Levator scapulae muscle (61, 62, 83, 188, 192). The interarticular region (223) and the midcarpal region (110) are linked to neighboring muscles (85) in the interclavicular area (101, 223).

The ligamentum teres of the femur appears to be mentioned ("Ligament... pue .did"). 

The interosseous membrane of the leg is found in the 393 region. The radiocarpal region (110, 130, 131) and acromioclavicular region (102, 103) are related to interosseous talocalcaneal points (399).

About the Author Andrew Biel is author of the best-selling textbook Trai! Guide to thc Body and Trail Guide to Movement. He has served on the faculties of Boulder College of Massage Therapy and Seattle Massage School and has taught Cadaver Studies for Bodyworkers at Bastyr Naturopathic University. He lives outside of Port Townsend, Washington, with his family - Lyn, Grace, and Elias. About the lllustrator Robin Dorn's background as a medical illustrator and licensed massage practitioner gives her the unique ability to create vibrant and detailed anatomical illustrations for Trail Guide textbooks. She exhibits her artwork on the West Coast aind in France. 435

text: Bodywork/Health Trail Guide to the Body was first published in 1997. Now in its 6th edition and with more than 900.000 copies sold, the textbook has become the gold standard for foundational musculoskeletal anatorny and palpation education. Used by nearly 2,500 programs worldwide and translated into 10 languages, Traif Guide to the Body prepares students for the skilled and knowledgeable practice of manual therapy in athletic training, massage therapy. occupational therapy, physical therapy. and various other bodywork, movement, and health care professions. Builds confidence on the learning journey. Trail Guide to the Body takes you on a confidence-building, step-by-step learning journey that includes well- defined trails through an abundance of musculoskeletal structures: 162 muscles, 206 bones, 33 ligaments, and 110 bony landmarks. Takes the mystery out of learning palpation, Every element of this best-selling textbook-from its visually rich and detailed anatomical illustrations confidence in the palpation skills needed to succeed in your future career. Comes with study tools to help you ace your exams. Trail Guide to the Body comes with exclusive access to an amazing repository of study tools. many included for free. Wherever you are, it's easy to access palpation videos, overlay images, interactive mobile apps, and a free online Trigger Point quick reference guide, available at booksofdiscovery.com. Where's your piriformis? Whether you're a student of athletic training, massage therapy, of manual therapy profession, Trail Guide to the Body is an essential 162 Muscles manual therapy resource! 206 Bones 33 Llgaments I. 110 Bony Landntarks Practlce palpation while watchlng videos! View free palpation videos Synergists, Tendons. now. Find your access code under Arteries, Veins, and Nerves the silver scratch-off box on the inside of the front cover. ANDREW BIEL, a pioneer in manual therapy education. has been creating engaging textbooks for students of the health sciences since 1997.
